source.title: Week 3 Readout — Retargeting (Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint)
source.body:
Owner: Ben Carter
Ops/Attribution: Tyler Benson
Sales exclusions + handoff rules: Jordan Blake / Stephanie Nguyen
Week: 3 (retargeting launch + first performance read)
Date range: 2026-01-28 → 2026-02-03 (7 days)

TL;DR
- Retargeting is live across LinkedIn + Google Display with conservative spend caps and strict sales opp exclusions.
- Best early signal: “Signup but no activation (no first request)” cohort shows strongest click intent and the most assisted activation lift.
- Weakest: “General site visitors” is cheap traffic but low downstream quality; keep it as small top-of-funnel retargeting only.
- Creative: “Hello World in 60s” / “First request snippet” variants outperform generic value-prop (latency/cost/reliability) on CTR and time-on-page.
- Attribution caveat: still early, and activation lag + cross-device means we should treat results as directional until next week.

Goal / success criteria (per sprint brief)
Primary: lift activation rate (% new signups completing first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days).
Retargeting-specific: reach stalled users efficiently without interfering with active enterprise opportunities.
Guardrails: conservative targeting, suppression of active opps + enterprise domains, frequency cap, and compliance alignment.

Channels & setup
- LinkedIn (single-image + short copy)
- Google Display (responsive display ads)
- Landing pages tested:
  A) Docs Quickstart (minimal “first request” snippet; aligns with quickstarts PR)
  B) Console sign-in deep link (for returning users; limited use due to auth friction)
- UTM convention (finalized in Slack):
  utm_source={linkedin|gdn}
  utm_medium=paid
  utm_campaign=lifecycle_growth_wk3_retargeting
  utm_content={audience}-{creative}-{lp}

Audiences (as launched)
A1: Site visitors (docs + pricing + homepage) — 30-day lookback
A2: Signup-no-activation — signed up but no “first successful request” within 72 hours
A3: Activated-but-no-return — first successful request completed, but no activity in last 7 days
Exclusions: active sales opps + flagged enterprise domains (weekly refresh), regulated/sensitive accounts as provided by Sales (manual list). Frequency cap set to avoid saturation.

Spend & efficiency summary (Week 3)
Notes:
- Spend caps were intentionally low for first week to validate exclusions + tracking.
- “Conversion” below is defined as “Signup” for A1 and “Return to console + API key created / first request” for A2/A3, depending on audience eligibility. We also look at assisted activation downstream.

Topline
- Total spend: $12,450
- Total clicks: 2,140
- Blended CPC: $5.82
- Blended CTR: 0.62%
- Blended (platform-reported) conversions: 116
- Blended CPA (platform-reported): $107.33

By channel
1) LinkedIn
- Spend: $8,200
- Impressions: 172,000
- Clicks: 1,070
- CTR: 0.62%
- Avg CPC: $7.66
- Platform conversions: 78
- CPA: $105.13

2) Google Display (GDN)
- Spend: $4,250
- Impressions: 173,500
- Clicks: 1,070
- CTR: 0.62%
- Avg CPC: $3.97
- Platform conversions: 38
- CPA: $111.84

Audience performance (early read)
Directional takeaways:
- A2 (Signup-no-activation) is the clear winner on downstream activation and lowest “wasted” clicks.
- A3 (Activated-but-no-return) is middling; seems sensitive to landing page choice (docs vs console deep link).
- A1 (Site visitors) is cheap scale but weak quality; keep budget small and optimize to “docs quickstart” only.

Table (blended across channels)
A1: Site visitors
- Spend: $3,600
- Clicks: 980
- CPC: $3.67
- Platform conversions (signup): 41
- CPA: $87.80
- Downstream activation note: low; many signups do not reach first request within 7 days.

A2: Signup-no-activation
- Spend: $5,950
- Clicks: 830
- CPC: $7.17
- Platform conversions (return + activation event): 58
- CPA: $102.59
- Downstream activation note: strongest assisted activation; visible lift in “first request within 24h of click” relative to holdout.

A3: Activated-but-no-return
- Spend: $2,900
- Clicks: 330
- CPC: $8.79
- Platform conversions (return session): 17
- CPA: $170.59
- Downstream activation note: modest; appears to re-engage “tinkerers” but not yet pushing to 100k token milestone.

Downstream activation impact (internal instrumentation)
Definitions (aligned with sprint KPIs):
- First successful request
- First 100k tokens within 7 days of signup
- D7 active usage (any successful request on day 7)

What we can say with current data:
- A2 shows a measurable bump in “first successful request within 24 hours after ad click” vs matched non-clickers.
- We are NOT yet confident on incremental impact on “first 100k tokens” due to lag and small N.
- Cross-device + ad blockers likely undercounting click-through attribution; we’re also monitoring view-through but treating it as softer signal.

Internal metric snapshots (week window, directional)
- A2 clickers: higher rate of completing “API key created” + “first request” within same day than A2 non-clickers.
- A1 clickers: signups happen, but activation remains the bottleneck; this looks like a docs/intent mismatch.
- A3 clickers: return sessions up slightly; needs better “next action” messaging (streaming, usage dashboard, scale guidance).

Creative performance & iteration notes
Creative themes tested (copy summarized):
C1: Developer-first activation
- “Make your first LLM request in ~60 seconds.”
- “Copy/paste the snippet. Streaming supported.”

C2: Value prop (latency/cost/reliability)
- “Lower latency, predictable costs, production-grade reliability.”

C3: Deployment flexibility (hosted/dedicated/private)
- “Hosted now, dedicated when you need it.”

Early results:
- C1 is outperforming across both channels in CTR and landing page engagement.
- C2 performs okay on LinkedIn but underperforms on GDN (likely too generic in that context).
- C3 underperforms for these audiences (feels too enterprise-y / too early in lifecycle).

Landing page learnings
- Docs Quickstart LP drives more “first request” completions than console deep link (console link has higher bounce due to auth + context switching).
- Recommendation: keep 80–90% of budget sending to Quickstart LP for A1/A2. For A3, test a “What to try next” page instead of console sign-in.

Sales exclusion / brand safety checks
- No reported incidents of retargeting active enterprise opps this week.
- Weekly exclusion list refresh cadence is working, but we had 1 near-miss: a domain that should be excluded was missing from the manual list until mid-week.
Action: Tyler + Isabelle to automate the refresh pull from CRM flags where possible, and keep a manual override list.

Attribution & measurement notes (important)
- Platform conversion definitions differ by audience; we’re over-indexing on internal activation events for decisioning.
- Known gaps:
  1) View-through conversions inflate perceived impact; we’re reporting them separately.
  2) Cross-device: LinkedIn click → desktop activation is hard to stitch.
  3) Small N: for A3 especially, week-to-week noise is high.
- Plan: rely on cohort-based internal reporting + holdout comparisons where feasible; Tyler to add a “retargeting_exposed” event property to improve analysis next week.

Budget & pacing
Initial cap (approved): conservative weekly cap with ability to scale if CPA is acceptable and guardrails hold.
Week 3 actual spend: $12.45k (under cap due to cautious ramp + audience sizes).
Proposal Week 4 allocation:
- 55% → A2 (signup-no-activation)
- 20% → A3 (activated-but-no-return) with new creative/LP
- 15% → A1 (site visitors) but only with C1 + Quickstart LP
- 10% → creative testing budget (new variants + format experiments)

Decisions / actions
Keep / scale
- Keep A2 and increase budget gradually (no more than +20–30% day-over-day) while monitoring CPA + activation lag.
- Keep LinkedIn as primary channel for A2; keep GDN for A1 (cheap reach) but narrow placements if quality remains low.

Iterate
- A3: replace enterprise-leaning creative; test “Enable streaming” + “See usage dashboard” messages and a purpose-built landing page.
- Landing pages: build a lightweight “Next steps after your first request” doc page (DevEx + Docs), link from ads for A3.

Kill / deprioritize
- Pause C3 (deployment flexibility) for retargeting audiences in this sprint; it’s not matching intent.

Risks / open questions
- Are we cannibalizing organic activation (i.e., users who would have activated anyway)? We need holdout-based incremental read.
- Audience definition hygiene: “Signup-no-activation within 72h” may be too short for some time zones / weekend signups; consider 96h and compare.

Owner assignments (next week)
- Tyler: tighten internal attribution and add exposed flags to event pipeline; document view-through vs click-through reporting.
- Ben: deliver creative brief v2 (C1 expansions + A3-specific copy), coordinate with Tessa for final exports.
- Jordan/Stephanie: confirm updated exclusion criteria and add any newly sensitive accounts (weekly).

Appendix: creative v2 ideas (rough)
A2 (signup-no-activation)
- Headline: “You’re 1 request away.”
- Body: “Create an API key + run the hello-world snippet. Streaming + structured output supported.”
- CTA: “Run first request”

A3 (activated-but-no-return)
- Headline: “Make it production-ready.”
- Body: “Enable streaming, track token cost, and set fallbacks. See the 5-minute checklist.”
- CTA: “View checklist”

Notes to self
- Don’t over-optimize to CTR. The only thing that matters is incremental activation + early retention without sales interference.
- Keep the messaging consistent with lifecycle emails + console nudges (Soojin review before scaling).

source.title: Experiment Readout Template (Lifecycle Growth Sprint)
source.body:
Purpose
- Standard weekly template for lifecycle growth experiments (email onboarding, in-console nudges, retargeting).
- Designed to force consistent definitions, comparable reporting, and clear keep/iterate/kill decisions.

How to use
- Fill this out for every experiment weekly (even if results are “too early”).
- If metrics changed due to instrumentation fixes (e.g., dedupe), explicitly call it out in “Data quality & caveats.”
- Keep the top summary short enough to paste into Slack + exec email.

============================================================
READOUT HEADER
============================================================
Experiment name:
- [Short name] (e.g., “Email v2: stalled signup → API key reminder” / “Console: checklist nudge on dashboard” / “Retargeting: signup-no-activation audience”)

Owner (DRI):
- [Name]

Contributors:
- [Names: marketing ops, product, eng, data]

Date range reported:
- Start: [YYYY-MM-DD]
- End: [YYYY-MM-DD]

Status:
- [Planning / Running / Paused / Ended]

Related tickets/PRs:
- Linear: [PM-xxx / ENG-xxxxx / DES-xxx]
- GitHub PR(s): [link/ID]
- Confluence spec (if applicable): [link/title]

============================================================
1) TL;DR (pasteable)
============================================================
Outcome:
- Decision: [KEEP / ITERATE / KILL / EXTEND TEST]
- Primary KPI impact: [e.g., +X% activation (first successful request within 7d)]
- Confidence: [High / Medium / Low] + 1-line why

What changed:
- [Variant summary in one line]

Key learnings:
- 1) [Learning]
- 2) [Learning]
- 3) [Learning]

Risks/guardrails:
- [Any deliverability, support load, spend overruns, compliance issues]

Next step (by date):
- [Action + owner + due date]

============================================================
2) Context & hypothesis
============================================================
User problem being solved:
- [What friction are we addressing? e.g., “new signups fail to create API key” / “users don’t know the minimal hello-world request”]

Hypothesis (testable statement):
- If we [change], then [target cohort] will [behavior change], resulting in [primary KPI movement] without violating guardrails.

Why we believe this (signals):
- [Qual feedback / support tickets / funnel drop-off / session replays / prior tests]

Primary KPI (must match sprint definition):
- Activation: % of new signups completing “first successful request” within 7 days
- Milestone: % of new signups reaching “first 100k tokens” within 7 days

Secondary KPIs (select what applies):
- Time-to-first-token (TTFT) / time-to-first-success
- D7 active usage, D30 active usage
- Upgrade intent / sales-assisted handoff signals (where applicable)
- Channel metrics:
  - Email: open rate, click-through rate, unsubscribe, complaint rate, bounce rate
  - Console nudge: impression→CTR, CTA completion rate, dismissal rate, downstream funnel completion
  - Retargeting: CTR, CPC, CPA (activation), view-through vs click-through notes, landing page conversion

Guardrails (select what applies):
- Deliverability thresholds not breached (bounce/complaint/unsub)
- Support tickets not spiking above baseline
- Retargeting spend within cap; no targeting of excluded audiences (active opps / enterprise domains / regulated lists)
- No GDPR/CAN-SPAM regressions; preferences respected

============================================================
3) Experiment setup
============================================================
Experiment type:
- [A/B test / holdout / stepped rollout / geo split / before-after]

Cohort definition:
- Inclusion:
  - [e.g., “new signups in last 14d” / “signup-no-activation for 24h” / “activated-but-no-return in 7d”]
- Exclusion:
  - [e.g., internal users, test accounts, blocked domains, active sales opps, regulated accounts, unsubscribed]

Randomization / assignment:
- Method: [feature flag cohort assignment / email split / ad platform split]
- Unit of randomization: [user / org / workspace]
- Holdout: [Y/N] If yes: [%] and where enforced

Variants:
- Control:
  - [What is the baseline experience?]
- Treatment A:
  - [What changed?]
- Treatment B (if any):
  - [What changed?]

Surfaces / placements:
- [Email #2 / console dashboard / API keys page / docs landing page / ad creative set]

Trigger logic (when does user see it?):
- [Exact trigger events + time delay + suppression rules]

Instrumentation & data sources:
- Event names used:
  - [e.g., signup_completed, api_key_created, first_request_succeeded, tokens_100k_reached]
- Key properties required:
  - [e.g., user_id, org_id, region, sdk_language, experiment_id, variant, timestamp]
- Dashboards/queries:
  - [Activation funnel dashboard link/name]
  - [SQL query name / dataset]

Sample size expectations:
- Planned MDE / sample size goal (if known): [ ]
- Actual sample size this period: [ ]

============================================================
4) Results (numbers + interpretation)
============================================================
A) Funnel metrics (recommended table)
- Report as absolute rate + delta vs control (and/or vs baseline).

Funnel stage | Control | Treatment | Delta | Notes
- Signup → API key created: [ ] | [ ] | [ ] | [ ]
- API key → first successful request (7d): [ ] | [ ] | [ ] | [ ]
- First success → first 100k tokens (7d): [ ] | [ ] | [ ] | [ ]
- D7 active usage: [ ] | [ ] | [ ] | [ ]

B) Primary KPI callout
- Activation (first successful request within 7d):
  - Control: [X%]
  - Treatment: [Y%]
  - Lift: [+/- Z pp] (relative: [+/- Z%])
  - Confidence: [stat sig if computed; otherwise directional + why]

C) Secondary metrics (channel-specific)
Email only:
- Open rate: [ ]
- Click-through rate: [ ]
- Unsubscribe rate: [ ]
- Complaint rate: [ ]
- Bounce rate: [ ]
- Top clicked CTA: [ ]

Console nudge only:
- Nudge impressions: [ ]
- CTR: [ ]
- CTA completion rate: [ ]
- Dismissal rate: [ ]
- Repeat exposure frequency (median): [ ]
- Noted UX issues: [ ]

Retargeting only:
- Spend: [$]
- CTR / CPC: [ ] / [$]
- Landing page CVR: [ ]
- Activation CPA (7d): [$]
- Audience segment performance: [best/worst + notes]

D) Breakdown cuts (choose 2–4 that matter)
- By region: [ ]
- By SDK language: [ ]
- By persona proxy (startup vs enterprise domain): [ ]
- By time-to-first-email / time-to-nudge exposure: [ ]

E) Data quality & caveats
- [Event gaps, regional tracking issues, dedupe changes, delayed attribution windows, bot traffic, ad platform reporting differences]
- Any changes during test window:
  - [Creative swapped, suppression rules updated, deliverability ramp adjustments, bugfix shipped]

F) Interpretation
- What do we think caused the result?
- What alternative explanations exist?
- Is this a “real user value” improvement vs measurement artifact?

============================================================
5) Guardrails / unintended consequences
============================================================
Deliverability (email):
- Domain reputation notes: [ ]
- Complaints/unsubs above threshold? [Y/N] Details: [ ]

Support + user sentiment:
- Support ticket count vs baseline: [ ]
- Common complaint themes: [ ]

Compliance/privacy:
- Preference center/unsubscribe respected? [Y/N]
- Any restricted accounts targeted? [Y/N]

Sales exclusions (retargeting):
- Active opp suppression working? [Y/N]
- Any known misses / suspected leakage? [ ]

============================================================
6) Decision & rationale
============================================================
Decision:
- [KEEP → roll out to 100% / ITERATE → new variant next week / KILL → revert / EXTEND → need more sample]

Decision criteria used:
- Primary KPI threshold: [ ]
- Guardrails: [met/not met]
- Effort to maintain: [low/med/high]

What we will ship or change:
- [Exact next action; include rollout plan if “keep”]

============================================================
7) Follow-ups / action items
============================================================
Actions (table format recommended)
- [Owner] — [Action] — [Due date] — [Link]

Backlog items created:
- [Linear ticket IDs]

Open questions:
- [What still isn’t understood?]

Appendix (optional)
- Screenshots/copy:
  - [Email subject/body]
  - [Nudge copy + placement]
  - [Ad creatives + landing pages]
- Query snippets / metric definitions used
- Notes from office hours / customer calls relevant to this test

source.title: Retargeting Creatives Brief (Lifecycle Growth Sprint) — Hosted API
source.content:
Slide 1 — Title / Context
Retargeting creatives brief (Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint)
Product: Redwood Inference — Hosted API
Sprint window: Week 3 launch (target: Jan 29–Feb 5)
Owner: Ben Carter (Growth)
Creative lead: Tessa Morgan (Design)
Messaging review: Soojin Lee
Attribution: Tyler Benson

Speaker notes:
We’re optimizing for self-serve activation + early retention. These are retargeting ads (not prospecting) aimed at developers who already touched Redwood (visited site / started signup / signed up but stalled).


Slide 2 — Goals (what “good” looks like)
Primary goal (activation lift):
- Increase % of retargeted users who reach “First successful request” within 7 days of signup
Secondary goals:
- Reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT) by driving users to the shortest path (API key + hello world request)
- Re-activate users who did first request but didn’t return (D7 activity)
Business constraints:
- No cannibalization of active enterprise opp motion (exclude active opps / sensitive accounts)

Success criteria (initial thresholds; update once baseline CPA is confirmed):
- CPA (activated user): within agreed cap from PM-670 (see budget ticket)
- CTR benchmark: aim for >= 0.9% on developer-first variants (channel-dependent)
- Landing page CVR (signup start or console login): aim for >= 6–10% on warm audiences
- Quality guardrail: no meaningful increase in support tickets tagged “onboarding/keys/auth” attributable to campaign

Speaker notes:
We will judge success on downstream activation events, not just clicks. If CTR is high but activation is flat, we iterate the landing page / CTA / audience rather than scaling spend.


Slide 3 — Target audiences (creative should map to stage)
Audience A: Site visitors (no signup)
- Who: visited docs/quickstart/pricing/model pages within lookback window
- Objective: “Come back + try it” (signup)

Audience B: Signup, no activation
- Who: created account but no API key OR no first successful request
- Objective: “Finish setup + make first request in 2 minutes”

Audience C: Activated, no return
- Who: first successful request completed but no activity after (e.g., 7–14 days)
- Objective: “Resume building” (streaming, evals/observability, usage dashboard)

Exclusions (must be honored in targeting logic + suppression lists):
- Active sales opps (HubSpot stage != Closed Lost) + accounts flagged by Sales
- Enterprise / regulated domains list where retargeting is disallowed (see audience definitions)
- Current paying Dedicated/Private customers (avoid mixed messaging)

Speaker notes:
Creative needs to clearly match the audience stage; we should not show “Create an API key” messaging to users who already created one.


Slide 4 — Messaging pillars (approved themes)
Pillar 1: Production-grade inference (reliability)
- “Predictable latency + uptime for real apps”
- Avoid absolute claims; no hard numbers unless validated in messaging guidelines

Pillar 2: Cost/performance control (economics)
- “Lower $/token via batching/caching/quantization options”
- Emphasize control/visibility, not ‘cheapest’

Pillar 3: Developer-first speed (time-to-value)
- “First request in minutes”
- “Copy/paste quickstart, streaming support, good errors”

Pillar 4 (secondary): Deployment flexibility
- “Hosted now, Dedicated/Private when you need it”

Tone / style rules:
- Speak to engineers (concrete verbs, avoid hype)
- Prefer “inference” / “latency” / “throughput” over vague “AI platform”
- Keep CTA direct (Start, Get API key, Make first request)

Speaker notes:
Soojin’s messaging review: lead with “time-to-value” for warm audiences; bring in reliability/cost as proof points.


Slide 5 — Creative directions (concepts)
Concept 1: “2-minute activation” (best for Audience B)
- Visual: simple flow diagram (Signup → API key → First request)
- Copy promise: “Make your first successful request in ~2 minutes” (approximate)
- CTA: “Finish setup” / “Make first request”

Concept 2: “Latency/cost knobs” (best for Audience C)
- Visual: console screenshot-style illustration (usage dashboard + latency/cost breakdown)
- Copy promise: “See token-level cost + latency. Tune batching/caching.”
- CTA: “Open Console”

Concept 3: “Production-ready Hosted API” (best for Audience A)
- Visual: developer-first API call snippet (curl / TypeScript) + small badge-style proof points
- Copy promise: “Ship LLM features with predictable inference”
- CTA: “Get started”

Concept 4: “Streaming UX” (Audience B/C)
- Visual: streaming response lines / terminal-style output
- Copy: “Enable streaming in one flag. Faster perceived latency.”
- CTA: “Try streaming”

Speaker notes:
We should request at least one “minimal, text-forward, dev” set and one more “product UI” set. Don’t over-index on abstract AI imagery.


Slide 6 — Copy bank (headlines, body, CTAs)
Headlines (mix-and-match):
1) Make your first LLM request in minutes
2) Hosted LLM inference you can ship
3) Predictable latency for production inference
4) See token-level cost + latency
5) Finish setup: create an API key
6) Try streaming responses (better UX)
7) Your eval is done—now ship it
8) Ready when you are: Hosted → Dedicated/Private

Body copy options (short):
A) Copy/paste a minimal snippet and get a successful response.
B) Monitor latency + cost per route. Tune batching/caching when you scale.
C) Built for production: streaming, fallbacks, rollouts, and observability.
D) Pick a model, send a request, and iterate fast.

CTA options (keep consistent with landing page):
- Get started
- Finish setup
- Create API key
- Make first request
- Open Console
- View quickstart

Compliance / policy note (copy):
- No mention of targeting based on sensitive attributes
- Avoid “We noticed you…” personalization in ad text

Speaker notes:
We will A/B at least two headline approaches: (1) time-to-value and (2) reliability/cost control.


Slide 7 — Formats + design requests (deliverables)
Channels:
- Primary: LinkedIn + X (retargeting)
- Optional (if we have bandwidth): Google Display (tight placements) for site-visitor retargeting

Deliverables requested from Design (minimum set):
1) Static images (3 concepts × 2 variants) = 6 total
2) One lightweight motion variant (optional) for “2-minute activation” concept

Ad sizes:
- 1200×628 (landscape)
- 1080×1080 (square)
- 1080×1920 (story) if we run story placements

Design constraints:
- Must be legible on mobile (big type, minimal text)
- Include Redwood logo + product name “Hosted API” or “Redwood API” where possible
- If using code snippet: ensure it’s readable, cropped tight, and not too small

Brand notes:
- Use Redwood brand palette + typography; keep backgrounds clean
- Avoid stock “robot/brain” visuals

Speaker notes:
Please include one version that is “pure dev”: mostly text + snippet + CTA, minimal decorative elements.


Slide 8 — Landing pages (map to audience) + CTA alignment
Audience A (site visitor, no signup):
- Landing: Hosted API quickstart (docs) OR product page (short, dev-first)
- CTA: “Get started”

Audience B (signup, no activation):
- Landing: Console login → onboarding checklist step (API key creation + hello world)
- CTA: “Finish setup” / “Make first request”

Audience C (activated, no return):
- Landing: Console usage dashboard OR “Enable streaming” docs section
- CTA: “Open Console” / “Try streaming”

Landing page requirements (non-negotiable):
- Loads fast, minimal friction
- Single primary CTA above the fold
- Clear 3-step path to first request (where applicable)

Speaker notes:
If we can’t deep-link reliably into console states, default to the docs quickstart + “Log in” CTA. Don’t send warm users to generic homepage.


Slide 9 — UTM conventions (for attribution + readouts)
UTM template:
- utm_source = linkedin | x | gdn
- utm_medium = paid_social | display
- utm_campaign = lifecycle_growth_sprint_wk3_retargeting
- utm_content = {concept}_{variant}_{size}
- utm_term = {audience_key}

Example:
utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid_social&utm_campaign=lifecycle_growth_sprint_wk3_retargeting&utm_content=activationflow_v1_1200x628&utm_term=signup_no_activation

Naming taxonomy (suggested):
- concept: activationflow | costlatency | hostedapi | streaming
- variant: v1 | v2 (copy differences)
- audience_key: site_visitor | signup_no_activation | activated_no_return

Speaker notes:
Tyler to confirm final UTM keys (see Slack thread). Creative filenames should match utm_content exactly to avoid reporting drift.


Slide 10 — Measurement plan (what we’ll report weekly)
Report at minimum by: channel × audience × creative concept
Top-of-funnel:
- Impressions, frequency, CTR, CPC
Mid-funnel:
- Landing page views, signup starts (Audience A), console logins (B/C)
Downstream activation (primary):
- “First successful request” within 7 days (attributed + blended)
- “First 100k tokens” within 7 days (secondary)
Retention (secondary):
- D7 active usage (for Audience C especially)

Experiment design notes:
- Keep a small holdout if feasible (platform-dependent)
- Control for seasonality by launching concepts in parallel

Speaker notes:
We will not call this a win based on CTR alone. The decision is “scale / iterate / stop” based on activated CPA and activation lift.


Slide 11 — Guardrails + failure modes to watch
Guardrails:
- Suppress active opps + restricted accounts weekly (Sales + CRM export)
- Cap frequency to avoid spammy impressions
- Stop/adjust if support load increases (tickets about email/ads confusion, auth issues)
- Ensure consent/compliance for any audience built from product events (see data handling policy)

Failure modes:
- Creative promises “2 minutes” but landing flow is longer → fix flow or soften claim
- Deep links break → users hit blank state → increase bounce
- Audience definition leaks active opps → immediate pause + postmortem

Speaker notes:
If we see high CTR + high bounce, assume mismatch between creative and landing page. Iterate quickly; don’t just swap images.


Slide 12 — Production checklist (handoff)
Inputs needed (Growth/Ops):
- Final audience definitions + suppression lists
- Final landing URLs per audience
- UTM keys finalized
- Budget caps + pacing plan

Creative acceptance criteria (Design):
- 3 concepts delivered in requested sizes
- At least 2 copy variants per concept
- Exported as PNG/JPG + source files
- Accessibility: high contrast, readable text, no tiny disclaimers

Review steps:
- Messaging review (Soojin)
- Sales exclusion sanity check (Jordan/Stephanie)
- Final ops QA in ad managers (Tyler)

Speaker notes:
Once assets are approved, we’ll launch with conservative spend for 48 hours, then reallocate to the best-performing audience+creative combinations.


Appendix — Open questions (parking lot)
1) Do we want to include “Dedicated/Private” in retargeting for Audience C, or keep focused on Hosted API value?
2) Which console page is the best default for Audience C (usage dashboard vs model catalog vs streaming docs)?
3) Do we have validated benchmark numbers we’re allowed to reference in ads (latency/cost claims)? If not, keep claims qualitative.
4) Should we create a dedicated “Activation landing page” optimized for B (signup-no-activation) instead of deep-linking into console?

preview:
Slide 1 — Title / Context
Retargeting creatives brief (Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint)
Product: Redwood Inference — Hosted API
Sprint window: Week 3 launch (target: Jan 29–Feb 5)
Owner: Ben Carter (Growth)
Creative lead: Tessa Morgan (Design)
Messaging review: Soojin Lee
Attribution: Tyler Benson

Speaker notes:
We’re optimizing for self-serve activation + early retention. These are retargeting ads (not prospecting) aimed at developers who already touched Redwood (visited site / started signup / signed up but stalled).


Slide 2 — Goals (what “good” looks like)
Primary goal (activation lift):
- Increase % of retargeted users who reach “First successful request” within 7 days of signup
Secondary goals:
- Reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT) by driving users to the shortest path (API key + hello world request)
- Re-activate users who did first request but didn’t return (D7 activity)
Business constraints:
- No cannibalization of active enterprise opp motion (exclude active opps / sensitive accounts)

Success criteria (initial thresholds; update once baseline CPA is confirmed):
- CPA (activated user): within agreed cap from PM-670 (see budget ticket)
- CTR benchmark: aim for >= 0.9% on developer-first variants (channel-dependent)
- Landing page CVR (signup start or console login): aim for >= 6–10% on warm audiences
- Quality guardrail: no meaningful increase in support tickets tagged “onboarding/keys/auth” attributable to campaign

Speaker notes:
We will judge success on downstream activation events, not just clicks. If CTR is high but activation is flat, we iterate the landing page / CTA / audience rather than scaling spend.


Slide 3 — Target audiences (creative should map to stage)
Audience A: Site visitors (no signup)
- Who: visited docs/quickstart/pricing/model pages within lookback window
- Objective: “Come back + try it” (signup)

Audience B: Signup, no activation
- Who: created account but no API key OR no first successful request
- Objective: “Finish setup + make first request in 2 minutes”

Audience C: Activated, no return
- Who: first successful request completed but no activity after (e.g., 7–14 days)
- Objective: “Resume building” (streaming, evals/observability, usage dashboard)

Exclusions (must be honored in targeting logic + suppression lists):
- Active sales opps (HubSpot stage != Closed Lost) + accounts flagged by Sales
- Enterprise / regulated domains list where retargeting is disallowed (see audience definitions)
- Current paying Dedicated/Private customers (avoid mixed messaging)

- Avoid absolute claims; no hard numbers unless validated in messaging guidelines

- “Lower $/token via batching/caching/quantization options”
- Emphasize control/visibility, not ‘cheapest’

Pillar 3: Developer-first speed (time-to-value)
- “First request in minutes”
- “Copy/paste quickstart, streaming support, good errors”

Pillar 4 (secondary): Deployment flexibility
- “Hosted now, Dedicated/Private when you need it”

Tone / style rules:
- Speak to engineers (concrete verbs, avoid hype)
- Prefer “inference” / “latency” / “throughput” over vague “AI platform”
- Keep CTA direct (Start, Get API key, Make first request)

- Suppress active opps + restricted accounts weekly (Sales + CRM export)
- Cap frequency to avoid spammy impressions
- Stop/adjust if support load increases (tickets about email/ads confusion, auth issues)
- Ensure consent/compliance for any audience built from product events (see data handling policy)

Failure modes:
- Creative promises “2 minutes” but landing flow is longer → fix flow or soften claim
- Deep links break → users hit blank state → increase bounce
- Audience definition leaks active opps → immediate pause + postmortem

Speaker notes:
If we see high CTR + high bounce, assume mismatch between creative and landing page. Iterate quickly; don’t just swap images.


Slide 12 — Production checklist (handoff)
Inputs needed (Growth/Ops):
- Final audience definitions + suppression lists
- Final landing URLs per audience
- UTM keys finalized
- Budget caps + pacing plan

Creative acceptance criteria (Design):
- 3 concepts delivered in requested sizes
- At least 2 copy variants per concept
- Exported as PNG/JPG + source files
- Accessibility: high contrast, readable text, no tiny disclaimers

Review steps:
- Messaging review (Soojin)
- Sales exclusion sanity check (Jordan/Stephanie)
- Final ops QA in ad managers (Tyler)

Speaker notes:
Once assets are approved, we’ll launch with conservative spend for 48 hours, then reallocate to the best-performing audience+creative combinations.


Appendix — Open questions (parking lot)
1) Do we want to include “Dedicated/Private” in retargeting for Audience C, or keep focused on Hosted API value?
2) Which console page is the best default for Audience C (usage dashboard vs model catalog vs streaming docs)?
3) Do we have validated benchmark numbers we’re allowed to reference in ads (latency/cost claims)? If not, keep claims qualitative.
4) Should we create a dedicated “Activation landing page” optimized for B (signup-no-activation) instead of deep-linking into console?

source.title: Week 1 Readout (Draft) — Onboarding Email v2 A/B
source.content:
Doc: Week 1 Readout (Draft) — Onboarding Email v2 A/B
Owner: Ben Carter
Date: 2026-01-21
Sprint: Lifecycle growth experiments (Hosted API activation/retention)

1) TL;DR (what we learned)
- Variant B (subject line + opening value prop) won on opens and click-to-console, and is trending positive on early activation leading indicators.
- Biggest drop-off continues to be between “signed up” → “API key created” for net-new users (esp. Gmail domains, likely hobbyists) and between “API key created” → “first request” for small teams that hit SDK/config friction.
- No deliverability red flags this week, but we’re still in conservative ramp mode; a small uptick in soft bounces came from a subset of EU addresses (needs monitoring).

2) Hypothesis + experiment summary
Hypothesis
- Clearer, performance-oriented messaging (latency/reliability) + a single “make your first request” CTA will increase activation within 7 days by reducing time-to-first-token.

What shipped (Week 1)
- Email #1 (sent ~15 minutes after signup) A/B test.
- Only changed: subject line + first 2 sentences + primary CTA label. Body structure and footer/compliance identical.
- Audience: new self-serve signups for Hosted API.
- Exclusions: unsubscribed, known enterprise opp domains (per Sales list v1), internal Redwood emails, and “already activated” within 10 minutes of signup (to avoid crediting email).

Variants
A (control-ish / v1 tone)
- Subject: “Welcome to Redwood — your API key is ready”
- Opening: oriented around getting started quickly
- CTA: “Create API key” (deep-link to console)

B (challenger / v2 performance tone)
- Subject: “Ship your first LLM request in minutes (fast + predictable)”
- Opening: explicit performance promise + reassurance (“production-grade latency + reliability, with simple API/SDK”)
- CTA: “Make your first request” (deep-link: console → API keys + quickstart snippet)

Success metrics (Week 1 readout window)
Primary (sprint KPI; measured at 7 days, will be final in Week 2+)
- Activation: % of new signups reaching (1) first successful request AND (2) first 100k tokens within 7 days.

Week 1 leading indicators (because 7-day is immature)
- Open rate
- Click-through rate (to console/docs)
- API key created within 24h
- First successful request within 72h
- Time-to-first-token (TTFT) for first successful request (directional)

3) Sample + methodology notes
- Send platform: SendGrid (marketing stream) with throttled ramp.
- Assignment: 50/50 at send-time using lifecycle_growth_email_ab_v2 flag.
- Attribution window for leading indicators:
  - “API key created within 24h” attributed if it occurs after send timestamp and within 24h of signup.
  - “First successful request within 72h” same logic; excludes requests from internal/test orgs.
- Data sources:
  - Send events (delivered/open/click)
  - Product events (signup, api_key_created, first_request_succeeded)
  - Joins via user_id + email hash; dedupe logic per ENG-10492 / PR-28431.

Caveats / known issues
- Open tracking is directionally useful but not perfect (Apple MPP).
- 7-day activation not fully baked (most of this cohort is 2–5 days old).
- We observed minor event lag in one region on 2026-01-20; Mei investigating pipeline delay (see PM-651 notes).

4) Results (overall)
Time window
- Cohort: signups from 2026-01-16 through 2026-01-20
- Readout generated: 2026-01-21 09:00 PT

Volume
- Eligible signups: 4,842
- Sent: 4,711 (A: 2,353 / B: 2,358)
- Delivered: 4,611 (A: 2,303 / B: 2,308)
- Delivery rate: 97.9% (A: 97.9% / B: 97.9%)

Engagement
- Open rate (delivered → opened)
  - A: 41.8%
  - B: 46.9%  (+5.1 pp)
- Click-through rate (delivered → clicked any link)
  - A: 6.2%
  - B: 7.5%   (+1.3 pp)
- Click-to-open rate (opened → clicked)
  - A: 14.8%
  - B: 16.0%  (+1.2 pp)

Leading activation indicators
- API key created within 24h (delivered cohort)
  - A: 28.4%
  - B: 30.1%  (+1.7 pp)
- First successful request within 72h (delivered cohort)
  - A: 12.6%
  - B: 13.5%  (+0.9 pp)

Time-to-value (directional)
- Median time from signup → first successful request (72h converters only)
  - A: 6h 18m
  - B: 5h 41m  (−37m)

Interpretation
- Variant B is clearly better at top-of-funnel email engagement.
- Activation lift is modest but directionally positive. Given immaturity of the cohort, we should keep B and continue collecting data through next week.

5) Segmentation cuts (what surprised us)
Segment definitions (quick)
- “New user / no org”: first-time signup, no prior workspace
- “Returning developer”: email seen before in product events (previous signup or key created historically)
- “Domain size proxy”: free email (gmail/outlook/yahoo) vs company domain
- “Geo”: NA vs EU vs APAC (based on signup region)

5.1 Free email domains (gmail/outlook/yahoo)
- Open rate lift was biggest here:
  - A: 39.5% opens
  - B: 46.2% opens
- Click lift exists but downstream API key creation is still low overall.
Takeaway: message resonates, but many users likely don’t make it into console / key creation flow.

5.2 Company domains
- Smaller open lift but better downstream conversion:
  - API key created within 24h
    - A: 33.7%
    - B: 35.4%
  - First successful request within 72h
    - A: 16.3%
    - B: 17.5%
Takeaway: these users have higher intent; email should optimize for “do the thing now” with fewer steps.

5.3 EU region
- Slightly lower delivery + higher soft bounces (still within normal):
  - Soft bounce rate: EU 1.9% vs overall 1.2%
- Engagement similar directionally, but smaller sample.
Action: monitor deliverability + ensure suppression rules and unsubscribe are functioning consistently across regions.

6) Qualitative feedback / support signals
- Support saw a small increase in “Where do I find my API key?” questions in community channels (not necessarily support tickets). This aligns with the key-creation drop-off.
- A few replies to the email asked for “copy/paste snippet” immediately in the email without needing console. That’s aligned with our “hello world activation snippet” work in quickstarts.

7) Decision + next iteration proposals
Decision (proposed)
- Ship Variant B as the default for Email #1 for the remainder of the sprint (keep holdout enabled).
- Keep A in a 10% shadow sample for one more week if we want to confirm stability across weekdays (optional; depends on tooling constraints).

Next iteration proposals (Week 2)
A) Improve click → key creation conversion
- Add a secondary, smaller CTA directly to “Create API key” on the same email (currently B pushes “Make your first request” which routes through the key step, but may feel indirect).
- Test microcopy in the email: “Step 1: Create an API key (30 seconds). Step 2: Run this curl/SDK snippet.”

B) Add “inline snippet” test (lightweight)
- Include a minimal curl + Typescript snippet in the email body (collapsed by default for mobile), with “Copy” buttons (or plain text).
- Hypothesis: reduces friction for developers who prefer staying in their editor.
- Risk: email length + deliverability; keep very compact.

C) Timing test (small)
- Current send is +15 minutes. Consider +3 minutes for a subset (e.g., 20%) to catch users while still in “setup mode”.
- Guardrail: do not send before email verification completes (avoid bounces/complaints).

D) Trigger for “stalled: no API key by 6 hours”
- Begin Email #2 to stalled cohort (copy already drafted in v2 doc).
- Keep it purely helpful, not salesy; include clear opt-out / preferences.

8) Open questions / asks
- Do we want to optimize Email #1 for “first successful request” (fast activation) or “first 100k tokens” (deeper engagement)? Soojin flagged that copy may need to hint at “scale” without overwhelming.
- Should we include performance proof points (benchmarks, latency numbers) this early, or keep the first email purely action-oriented?
- Confirm with Sales: enterprise opp exclusion list v1 is working; need weekly refresh cadence (Jordan + Isabelle).

9) Deliverability + compliance checklist (Week 1)
- Unsubscribe link present and functioning (spot-checked).
- Complaint rate: 0.05% (below 0.1% guardrail).
- Hard bounce rate: 0.4% (stable).
- Soft bounce rate: 1.2% (watch EU).
- Ramp: capped at ~1,000 sends/day initially; increased to ~1,400/day by end of week.

10) Appendix: exact links/UTMs used
- Console deep link: /console/onboarding?step=first_request
- Docs link: /docs/quickstart?utm_source=lifecycle_email&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2_email1
- UTM convention: utm_source=lifecycle_email, utm_medium=email, utm_campaign=onboarding_v2, utm_content=variant_a|variant_b

11) Appendix: next week owners
- Ben Carter: final decision + copy updates + launch
- Tyler Benson: UTM / attribution QA + dashboard checks
- Mei Lin: funnel query validation + latency on event joins
- Soojin Lee: copy review for next iteration
- Ethan Park / Benji Okafor: ensure flag + holdout stable; verify no cross-cohort contamination

Notes to self (Ben)
- When we report externally (exec summary), do not overclaim activation lift until 7-day window closes.
- Pull one screenshot of the email client rendering on mobile; we may be losing clicks due to CTA fold placement.

8) Open questions / asks
- Do we want to optimize Email #1 for “first successful request” (fast activation) or “first 100k tokens” (deeper engagement)? Soojin flagged that copy may need to hint at “scale” without overwhelming.
- Should we include performance proof points (benchmarks, latency numbers) this early, or keep the first email purely action-oriented?
- Confirm with Sales: enterprise opp exclusion list v1 is working; need weekly refresh cadence (Jordan + Isabelle).

9) Deliverability + compliance checklist (Week 1)
- Unsubscribe link present and functioning (spot-checked).
- Complaint rate: 0.05% (below 0.1% guardrail).
- Hard bounce rate: 0.4% (stable).
- Soft bounce rate: 1.2% (watch EU).
- Ramp: capped at ~1,000 sends/day initially; increased to ~1,400/day by end of week.

10) Appendix: exact links/UTMs used
- Console deep link: /console/onboarding?step=first_request
- Docs link: /docs/quickstart?utm_source=lifecycle_email&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2_email1
- UTM convention: utm_source=lifecycle_email, utm_medium=email, utm_campaign=onboarding_v2, utm_content=variant_a|variant_b

11) Appendix: next week owners
- Ben Carter: final decision + copy updates + launch
- Tyler Benson: UTM / attribution QA + dashboard checks
- Mei Lin: funnel query validation + latency on event joins
- Soojin Lee: copy review for next iteration
- Ethan Park / Benji Okafor: ensure flag + holdout stable; verify no cross-cohort contamination

Notes to self (Ben)
- When we report externally (exec summary), do not overclaim activation lift until 7-day window closes.
- Pull one screenshot of the email client rendering on mobile; we may be losing clicks due to CTA fold placement.

source.title: Onboarding email copy variants v2 (Hosted API) — subjects, bodies, CTAs + review notes
source.content:
Last updated: 2026-01-18 (Ben)

Purpose
- Working copy doc for Onboarding Email v2 (Hosted API).
- Includes: subject line sets, body variants, CTA tests, and rationale.
- Includes reviewer comments + open questions.

Primary goal
- Lift activation: % of new signups completing (1) first successful request and (2) first 100k tokens within 7 days.

Secondary goals
- Reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT) by pushing toward “first request” snippet.
- Improve D7 return (nudge to usage dashboard + streaming).

Guiding messaging (from guidelines)
- Developer-first, production-grade inference.
- Emphasize: predictable latency, cost controls, reliability. Avoid overpromising.
- Keep “fast to first request” as the hero, with credibility cues (observability, fallbacks, dedicated/private path later).

Personalization + tokens (marketing ops)
- Use merge tags where available:
  - {{first_name}} (fallback: “there”)
  - {{org_name}} (fallback: “your team”)
  - {{api_key_created}} (boolean; for conditional block)
  - {{console_url}}
  - {{quickstart_url}}
  - {{docs_auth_url}}
  - {{support_email}}

Global compliance/footer
- Include physical address + unsubscribe link (SendGrid-managed)
- CAN-SPAM/GDPR: do not imply consent beyond product signup.
- Avoid “free money / guaranteed / no risk / act now” phrasing.

Links (draft)
- Console: https://console.redwoodinference.com/ (append UTM)
- API keys page: https://console.redwoodinference.com/settings/api-keys (append UTM)
- Quickstart “first request” snippet: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstart/first-request (append UTM)
- Help: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/support

UTM convention (Tyler)
- utm_source=lifecycle
- utm_medium=email
- utm_campaign=onboarding_v2
- utm_content={{email_name}}_{{variant}}

--------------------------------------------
Email 0: Signup confirmation / “Welcome” (send ~5 min after signup)
Audience
- New signups (all), but suppress if already activated within first 5 minutes (to avoid awkwardness).

Subject line options (pick 1–2 for A/B)
S0-A (Speed): “Welcome to Redwood — make your first request in 5 minutes”
  Rationale: sets expectation + time-bound outcome.
  [Soojin] “5 minutes is okay if we can back it up with the snippet. Avoid sounding like a marketing claim.”
S0-B (Neutral): “Your Redwood API is ready”
  Rationale: simple, low-spam, transactional vibe.
S0-C (Value prop): “Ship reliable LLM inference (without tuning your own stack)”
  Rationale: stronger positioning; risk: too “pitchy.”
S0-D (Curiosity): “Copy your API key → send your first prompt”
  Rationale: direct next step.

Preheader options
P0-1: “Get an API key and run a hello-world request.”
P0-2: “Quickstart snippet inside. Streaming supported.”
P0-3: “Console walkthrough + docs links.”

Body copy variants
Variant S0-Body-A (action-first)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Welcome to Redwood Inference. The fastest path to value is a single successful request.

1) Create an API key
2) Run the quickstart snippet
3) See tokens + latency in the Console

CTA: Create an API key
Secondary: View the quickstart

If you get stuck, reply to this email or reach us at {{support_email}}.
---

Variant S0-Body-B (credibility-first)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Redwood helps engineering teams run LLM inference with predictable latency and production-grade reliability across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Start here:
- Create an API key (1 minute)
- Make your first request (copy/paste snippet)

CTA: Make your first request
Secondary: API key settings
---

CTA label tests
- CTA-1: “Create API key” (good for non-activated cohort)
- CTA-2: “Make first request” (good if API key exists)
- CTA-3: “Open Console” (generic, might underperform)

Conditional block idea (Ethan/Paula)
- If {{api_key_created}} = true, skip API key step and lead with quickstart snippet.

Notes / potential issues
- Ensure we do not send both Email 0 and Email 1 in quick succession if triggers overlap.
- Keep this email short; avoid multiple competing CTAs.

--------------------------------------------
Email 1: “No API key yet” reminder (send ~20–24h after signup if no API key)
Audience
- Signed up, no API key created.

Subject line options
S1-A: “Next step: create your Redwood API key”
S1-B: “Still need an API key?”
S1-C: “Finish setup in the Console (API key)”
S1-D: “Get unblocked: API key + first request”
  [Tyler] “Avoid ‘unblocked’ if deliverability flags it; watch spam scoring.”

Preheader
P1-1: “It’s the only required step before your first request.”
P1-2: “Copy/paste snippet included after key creation.”

Body variants
Variant S1-Body-A (minimal)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Looks like you haven’t created an API key yet. That’s the only required setup step.

CTA: Create your API key
Secondary: Quickstart
---

Variant S1-Body-B (value + reassurance)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Once you have an API key, you can:
- Stream responses
- Track token usage + latency
- Control cost via batching/caching settings (later — first get a request working)

CTA: Create your API key
Secondary: View the “first request” snippet

[Soojin] Suggest removing “batching/caching settings” from this email; too advanced for Day 1.
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “Create API key”
- CTA-2: “Open API key settings”

Open question
- Should we include 1-line troubleshooting (“If you don’t see API Keys, check you’re in the right org”)?
  [Marissa] Console already handles org switcher but people get lost.

--------------------------------------------
Email 2: “API key created, no first request” (send ~24h after API key creation if no successful request)
Audience
- Has API key but hasn’t completed first successful request.

Subject options
S2-A: “Copy/paste your first request (2 minutes)”
S2-B: “Your API key is set — run a hello-world request”
S2-C: “Make your first successful call to Redwood”
S2-D: “Need help with your first request?”

Preheader
P2-1: “Here’s a minimal snippet you can run locally.”
P2-2: “Includes streaming example.”

Body variants
Variant S2-Body-A (snippet-forward)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

You’re set up with an API key. Next: run a minimal request.

Quickstart (recommended):
1) Copy the snippet
2) Set REDWOOD_API_KEY
3) Run it and confirm you see a response

CTA: View the first-request snippet
Secondary: Open Console

Troubleshooting:
- 401/403: API key missing/invalid
- Timeout: check outbound network / try smaller max_tokens

[Paula] If we mention errors, make sure they match actual SDK/runtime errors.
---

Variant S2-Body-B (streaming hook)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Want faster time-to-value? Turn on streaming and see tokens arrive immediately.

CTA: Run the streaming quickstart
Secondary: Standard quickstart

[Soojin] “faster time-to-value” ok, but don’t imply “faster inference.”
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “View quickstart”
- CTA-2: “Run streaming example”

Open questions
- Do we embed the snippet inline (code block) vs link out?
  - Pro: fewer clicks.
  - Con: formatting issues in email clients; harder to update.
  [Tyler] Link-out helps attribution, but inline might lift activation.

--------------------------------------------
Email 3: “First success” reinforcement (send ~1–2h after first successful request)
Audience
- Completed first successful request.
Goal
- Encourage next milestone: first 100k tokens + D7 retention (return usage).

Subject options
S3-A: “Nice — you made your first request”
S3-B: “You’re live. Next: usage + latency visibility”
S3-C: “Track tokens and latency in the Console”

Preheader
P3-1: “Here’s how to monitor cost/latency and avoid surprises.”

Body variants
Variant S3-Body-A (celebrate + next steps)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

You just sent your first successful request — nice.

Next best steps:
1) Turn on streaming (if you haven’t)
2) Check your usage dashboard (tokens + latency)
3) Save your model/version choice for repeatable results

CTA: View usage dashboard
Secondary: Enable streaming

[Soojin] Keep “nice” but avoid anything too hype-y. Also make sure we can actually deep-link “Enable streaming.”
---

Variant S3-Body-B (reliability angle)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Great — your first request worked.

If you’re moving toward production, you’ll likely care about:
- Latency and tail performance
- Reliability + graceful fallback
- Cost per 1k tokens

Start with the usage dashboard to see what your workload looks like.

CTA: Open usage dashboard
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “Open usage dashboard”
- CTA-2: “See latency & tokens”

Open question
- Do we mention Dedicated/Private here or keep it PLG-only?
  [Ben] Keep it out of v2 lifecycle emails for now; add later when upgrade intent signals exist.

--------------------------------------------
Email 4: “Stalled after first success / no return” (send day 5–6 if first request done but no activity in 72h)
Audience
- Activated once, then stopped.
Goal
- Get them back for a second session; identify friction.

Subject options
S4-A: “Need help getting to your next milestone?”
S4-B: “Anything blocking your Redwood integration?”
S4-C: “Quick check-in: want a working example app?”

Preheader
P4-1: “Minimal chat app template + common gotchas.”

Body variants
Variant S4-Body-A (help-first)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Noticed you got a request working, but haven’t come back yet.

If you’re stuck, here are the top 3 issues we see:
- API key not set in the environment where you’re running
- Using the wrong base URL / model name
- Not seeing streaming output due to client buffering

Want a concrete starting point? We have a minimal example app you can run locally.

CTA: Use the minimal chat app template
Secondary: Reply with your error and we’ll help

[Michael Grant] “Noticed you…” is generally okay for product lifecycle, but we should avoid anything that feels like surveillance. Consider “If you haven’t had a chance to continue…”
---

Variant S4-Body-B (softened language)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

If you haven’t had a chance to continue your Redwood setup, a minimal template can help you get to a real integration faster.

CTA: Open the minimal template
Secondary: View docs
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “Use the minimal template”
- CTA-2: “Get an example app”

Open questions
- Do we route replies to Support or Growth? (Ben currently gets them; may not scale.)
- Should we add a 1-question micro-survey (“What blocked you?”) — yes/no + free text?
  [Tyler] Survey links often reduce deliverability; keep for later.

--------------------------------------------
Subject line “bank” (extras for rotation / future tests)
- “Your first request is one snippet away”
- “Set up Redwood in the Console”
- “Make your LLM endpoint feel predictable”
- “From prototype → production inference (step-by-step)”
- “Latency, cost, reliability — start measuring now”

Words/phrases to avoid (deliverability + tone)
- “guaranteed”, “no risk”, “act now”, “limited time”, “urgent”
- too many exclamation points
- “Free credits” in subject (OK in body if needed; TBD)

--------------------------------------------
Experiment matrix (rough)
Email 0
- Test: Subject S0-A vs S0-B
- Test: Body A vs Body B
- Primary metric: activation within 24h (first request)

Email 2
- Test: inline snippet vs link-out (if feasible)
- Primary metric: first successful request within 24h of send

Email 3
- Test: CTA “usage dashboard” vs “enable streaming” ordering
- Primary metric: progress to 100k tokens within 7d + D7 return

Measurement notes
- Attribute to email send using holdout + last-touch within 24h window (confirm with Tyler)
- Track opens/clicks but optimize for activation lift.
- Ensure dedupe on signup and first_request events (see ENG-10492 + PR-28521 fix)

--------------------------------------------
Reviewer comments (captured, not yet resolved)
[Soojin Lee]
- Prefer “predictable latency” over “fastest latency.”
- Reduce advanced features on Day 1; keep steps linear.
- Keep voice developer-to-developer, not “marketing brochure.”

[Tyler Benson]
- Don’t overload with links; keep 1 primary CTA.
- Make UTMs consistent; avoid multiple utm_content patterns.
- Watch spam words; test deliverability with seed lists.

[Marissa Cole]
- Confirm deep links exist for Console pages referenced.
- Ensure email copy matches in-console nudge copy (CTA naming consistency).

[Paula Mendes]
- Double-check event-based triggers and timing: we need correct “api_key_created_at” and “first_successful_request_at.”
- If we add troubleshooting bullets, ensure they match what SDK actually returns.

[Michael Grant]
- Consider softening behavioral language (“noticed you…”).
- Ensure unsubscribe is present and preferences honored (ENG-10518).

--------------------------------------------
Open questions / TODO
1) Inline snippet vs link-out: decide by 2026-01-20 (needs template constraints + attribution needs).
2) Finalize CTA names to match Console: “Create API key” vs “API Keys” vs “Open Console.”
3) Decide if we mention streaming in Email 0 or push to Email 3.
4) Confirm we can send Email 3 (post-first-success) without confusing support load.
5) Ensure suppression rules for enterprise opps? (Probably not applicable for onboarding lifecycle, but confirm with Jordan/Stephanie if needed.)

Draft landing pages/docs alignment
- Docs PR (redwood-docs/pr-913) should use the same terms as the CTA.
- Quickstart PR (redwood-quickstarts/pr-412) needs to be the canonical “first request” referenced here.

Notes to self (Ben)
- The copy is still too long in Email 2 if we add troubleshooting. Keep it tight.
- If we keep “5 minutes” claim, make sure snippet really is minimal and doesn’t require extra SDK setup.
