Reviewer comments (captured, not yet resolved)
[Soojin Lee]
- Prefer “predictable latency” over “fastest latency.”
- Reduce advanced features on Day 1; keep steps linear.
- Keep voice developer-to-developer, not “marketing brochure.”

[Tyler Benson]
- Don’t overload with links; keep 1 primary CTA.
- Make UTMs consistent; avoid multiple utm_content patterns.
- Watch spam words; test deliverability with seed lists.

[Marissa Cole]
- Confirm deep links exist for Console pages referenced.
- Ensure email copy matches in-console nudge copy (CTA naming consistency).

[Paula Mendes]
- Double-check event-based triggers and timing: we need correct “api_key_created_at” and “first_successful_request_at.”
- If we add troubleshooting bullets, ensure they match what SDK actually returns.

[Michael Grant]
- Consider softening behavioral language (“noticed you…”).
- Ensure unsubscribe is present and preferences honored (ENG-10518).

--------------------------------------------
Open questions / TODO
1) Inline snippet vs link-out: decide by 2026-01-20 (needs template constraints + attribution needs).
2) Finalize CTA names to match Console: “Create API key” vs “API Keys” vs “Open Console.”
3) Decide if we mention streaming in Email 0 or push to Email 3.
4) Confirm we can send Email 3 (post-first-success) without confusing support load.
5) Ensure suppression rules for enterprise opps? (Probably not applicable for onboarding lifecycle, but confirm with Jordan/Stephanie if needed.)

Draft landing pages/docs alignment
- Docs PR (redwood-docs/pr-913) should use the same terms as the CTA.
- Quickstart PR (redwood-quickstarts/pr-412) needs to be the canonical “first request” referenced here.

Notes to self (Ben)
- The copy is still too long in Email 2 if we add troubleshooting. Keep it tight.
- If we keep “5 minutes” claim, make sure snippet really is minimal and doesn’t require extra SDK setup.
text:
Last updated: 2026-01-18 (Ben)

Purpose
- Working copy doc for Onboarding Email v2 (Hosted API).
- Includes: subject line sets, body variants, CTA tests, and rationale.
- Includes reviewer comments + open questions.

Primary goal
- Lift activation: % of new signups completing (1) first successful request and (2) first 100k tokens within 7 days.

Secondary goals
- Reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT) by pushing toward “first request” snippet.
- Improve D7 return (nudge to usage dashboard + streaming).

Guiding messaging (from guidelines)
- Developer-first, production-grade inference.
- Emphasize: predictable latency, cost controls, reliability. Avoid overpromising.
- Keep “fast to first request” as the hero, with credibility cues (observability, fallbacks, dedicated/private path later).

Personalization + tokens (marketing ops)
- Use merge tags where available:
  - {{first_name}} (fallback: “there”)
  - {{org_name}} (fallback: “your team”)
  - {{api_key_created}} (boolean; for conditional block)
  - {{console_url}}
  - {{quickstart_url}}
  - {{docs_auth_url}}
  - {{support_email}}

Global compliance/footer
- Include physical address + unsubscribe link (SendGrid-managed)
- CAN-SPAM/GDPR: do not imply consent beyond product signup.
- Avoid “free money / guaranteed / no risk / act now” phrasing.

Links (draft)
- Console: https://console.redwoodinference.com/ (append UTM)
- API keys page: https://console.redwoodinference.com/settings/api-keys (append UTM)
- Quickstart “first request” snippet: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstart/first-request (append UTM)
- Help: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/support

UTM convention (Tyler)
- utm_source=lifecycle
- utm_medium=email
- utm_campaign=onboarding_v2
- utm_content={{email_name}}_{{variant}}

--------------------------------------------
Email 0: Signup confirmation / “Welcome” (send ~5 min after signup)
Audience
- New signups (all), but suppress if already activated within first 5 minutes (to avoid awkwardness).

Subject line options (pick 1–2 for A/B)
S0-A (Speed): “Welcome to Redwood — make your first request in 5 minutes”
  Rationale: sets expectation + time-bound outcome.
  [Soojin] “5 minutes is okay if we can back it up with the snippet. Avoid sounding like a marketing claim.”
S0-B (Neutral): “Your Redwood API is ready”
  Rationale: simple, low-spam, transactional vibe.
S0-C (Value prop): “Ship reliable LLM inference (without tuning your own stack)”
  Rationale: stronger positioning; risk: too “pitchy.”
S0-D (Curiosity): “Copy your API key → send your first prompt”
  Rationale: direct next step.

Preheader options
P0-1: “Get an API key and run a hello-world request.”
P0-2: “Quickstart snippet inside. Streaming supported.”
P0-3: “Console walkthrough + docs links.”

Body copy variants
Variant S0-Body-A (action-first)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Welcome to Redwood Inference. The fastest path to value is a single successful request.

1) Create an API key
2) Run the quickstart snippet
3) See tokens + latency in the Console

CTA: Create an API key
Secondary: View the quickstart

If you get stuck, reply to this email or reach us at {{support_email}}.
---

Variant S0-Body-B (credibility-first)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Redwood helps engineering teams run LLM inference with predictable latency and production-grade reliability across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Start here:
- Create an API key (1 minute)
- Make your first request (copy/paste snippet)

CTA: Make your first request
Secondary: API key settings
---

CTA label tests
- CTA-1: “Create API key” (good for non-activated cohort)
- CTA-2: “Make first request” (good if API key exists)
- CTA-3: “Open Console” (generic, might underperform)

Conditional block idea (Ethan/Paula)
- If {{api_key_created}} = true, skip API key step and lead with quickstart snippet.

Notes / potential issues
- Ensure we do not send both Email 0 and Email 1 in quick succession if triggers overlap.
- Keep this email short; avoid multiple competing CTAs.

--------------------------------------------
Email 1: “No API key yet” reminder (send ~20–24h after signup if no API key)
Audience
- Signed up, no API key created.

Subject line options
S1-A: “Next step: create your Redwood API key”
S1-B: “Still need an API key?”
S1-C: “Finish setup in the Console (API key)”
S1-D: “Get unblocked: API key + first request”
  [Tyler] “Avoid ‘unblocked’ if deliverability flags it; watch spam scoring.”

Preheader
P1-1: “It’s the only required step before your first request.”
P1-2: “Copy/paste snippet included after key creation.”

Body variants
Variant S1-Body-A (minimal)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Looks like you haven’t created an API key yet. That’s the only required setup step.

CTA: Create your API key
Secondary: Quickstart
---

Variant S1-Body-B (value + reassurance)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Once you have an API key, you can:
- Stream responses
- Track token usage + latency
- Control cost via batching/caching settings (later — first get a request working)

CTA: Create your API key
Secondary: View the “first request” snippet

[Soojin] Suggest removing “batching/caching settings” from this email; too advanced for Day 1.
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “Create API key”
- CTA-2: “Open API key settings”

Open question
- Should we include 1-line troubleshooting (“If you don’t see API Keys, check you’re in the right org”)?
  [Marissa] Console already handles org switcher but people get lost.

--------------------------------------------
Email 2: “API key created, no first request” (send ~24h after API key creation if no successful request)
Audience
- Has API key but hasn’t completed first successful request.

Subject options
S2-A: “Copy/paste your first request (2 minutes)”
S2-B: “Your API key is set — run a hello-world request”
S2-C: “Make your first successful call to Redwood”
S2-D: “Need help with your first request?”

Preheader
P2-1: “Here’s a minimal snippet you can run locally.”
P2-2: “Includes streaming example.”

Body variants
Variant S2-Body-A (snippet-forward)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

You’re set up with an API key. Next: run a minimal request.

Quickstart (recommended):
1) Copy the snippet
2) Set REDWOOD_API_KEY
3) Run it and confirm you see a response

CTA: View the first-request snippet
Secondary: Open Console

Troubleshooting:
- 401/403: API key missing/invalid
- Timeout: check outbound network / try smaller max_tokens

[Paula] If we mention errors, make sure they match actual SDK/runtime errors.
---

Variant S2-Body-B (streaming hook)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Want faster time-to-value? Turn on streaming and see tokens arrive immediately.

CTA: Run the streaming quickstart
Secondary: Standard quickstart

[Soojin] “faster time-to-value” ok, but don’t imply “faster inference.”
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “View quickstart”
- CTA-2: “Run streaming example”

Open questions
- Do we embed the snippet inline (code block) vs link out?
  - Pro: fewer clicks.
  - Con: formatting issues in email clients; harder to update.
  [Tyler] Link-out helps attribution, but inline might lift activation.

--------------------------------------------
Email 3: “First success” reinforcement (send ~1–2h after first successful request)
Audience
- Completed first successful request.
Goal
- Encourage next milestone: first 100k tokens + D7 retention (return usage).

Subject options
S3-A: “Nice — you made your first request”
S3-B: “You’re live. Next: usage + latency visibility”
S3-C: “Track tokens and latency in the Console”

Preheader
P3-1: “Here’s how to monitor cost/latency and avoid surprises.”

Body variants
Variant S3-Body-A (celebrate + next steps)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

You just sent your first successful request — nice.

Next best steps:
1) Turn on streaming (if you haven’t)
2) Check your usage dashboard (tokens + latency)
3) Save your model/version choice for repeatable results

CTA: View usage dashboard
Secondary: Enable streaming

[Soojin] Keep “nice” but avoid anything too hype-y. Also make sure we can actually deep-link “Enable streaming.”
---

Variant S3-Body-B (reliability angle)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Great — your first request worked.

If you’re moving toward production, you’ll likely care about:
- Latency and tail performance
- Reliability + graceful fallback
- Cost per 1k tokens

Start with the usage dashboard to see what your workload looks like.

CTA: Open usage dashboard
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “Open usage dashboard”
- CTA-2: “See latency & tokens”

Open question
- Do we mention Dedicated/Private here or keep it PLG-only?
  [Ben] Keep it out of v2 lifecycle emails for now; add later when upgrade intent signals exist.

--------------------------------------------
Email 4: “Stalled after first success / no return” (send day 5–6 if first request done but no activity in 72h)
Audience
- Activated once, then stopped.
Goal
- Get them back for a second session; identify friction.

Subject options
S4-A: “Need help getting to your next milestone?”
S4-B: “Anything blocking your Redwood integration?”
S4-C: “Quick check-in: want a working example app?”

Preheader
P4-1: “Minimal chat app template + common gotchas.”

Body variants
Variant S4-Body-A (help-first)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

Noticed you got a request working, but haven’t come back yet.

If you’re stuck, here are the top 3 issues we see:
- API key not set in the environment where you’re running
- Using the wrong base URL / model name
- Not seeing streaming output due to client buffering

Want a concrete starting point? We have a minimal example app you can run locally.

CTA: Use the minimal chat app template
Secondary: Reply with your error and we’ll help

[Michael Grant] “Noticed you…” is generally okay for product lifecycle, but we should avoid anything that feels like surveillance. Consider “If you haven’t had a chance to continue…”
---

Variant S4-Body-B (softened language)
---
Hi {{first_name}},

If you haven’t had a chance to continue your Redwood setup, a minimal template can help you get to a real integration faster.

CTA: Open the minimal template
Secondary: View docs
---

CTA tests
- CTA-1: “Use the minimal template”
- CTA-2: “Get an example app”

Open questions
- Do we route replies to Support or Growth? (Ben currently gets them; may not scale.)
- Should we add a 1-question micro-survey (“What blocked you?”) — yes/no + free text?
  [Tyler] Survey links often reduce deliverability; keep for later.

--------------------------------------------
Subject line “bank” (extras for rotation / future tests)
- “Your first request is one snippet away”
- “Set up Redwood in the Console”
- “Make your LLM endpoint feel predictable”
- “From prototype → production inference (step-by-step)”
- “Latency, cost, reliability — start measuring now”

Words/phrases to avoid (deliverability + tone)
- “guaranteed”, “no risk”, “act now”, “limited time”, “urgent”
- too many exclamation points
- “Free credits” in subject (OK in body if needed; TBD)

--------------------------------------------
Experiment matrix (rough)
Email 0
- Test: Subject S0-A vs S0-B
- Test: Body A vs Body B
- Primary metric: activation within 24h (first request)

Email 2
- Test: inline snippet vs link-out (if feasible)
- Primary metric: first successful request within 24h of send

Email 3
- Test: CTA “usage dashboard” vs “enable streaming” ordering
- Primary metric: progress to 100k tokens within 7d + D7 return

Measurement notes
- Attribute to email send using holdout + last-touch within 24h window (confirm with Tyler)
- Track opens/clicks but optimize for activation lift.
- Ensure dedupe on signup and first_request events (see ENG-10492 + PR-28521 fix)

--------------------------------------------
Reviewer comments (captured, not yet resolved)
[Soojin Lee]
- Prefer “predictable latency” over “fastest latency.”
- Reduce advanced features on Day 1; keep steps linear.
- Keep voice developer-to-developer, not “marketing brochure.”

[Tyler Benson]
- Don’t overload with links; keep 1 primary CTA.
- Make UTMs consistent; avoid multiple utm_content patterns.
- Watch spam words; test deliverability with seed lists.

[Marissa Cole]
- Confirm deep links exist for Console pages referenced.
- Ensure email copy matches in-console nudge copy (CTA naming consistency).

[Paula Mendes]
- Double-check event-based triggers and timing: we need correct “api_key_created_at” and “first_successful_request_at.”
- If we add troubleshooting bullets, ensure they match what SDK actually returns.

[Michael Grant]
- Consider softening behavioral language (“noticed you…”).
- Ensure unsubscribe is present and preferences honored (ENG-10518).

--------------------------------------------
Open questions / TODO
1) Inline snippet vs link-out: decide by 2026-01-20 (needs template constraints + attribution needs).
2) Finalize CTA names to match Console: “Create API key” vs “API Keys” vs “Open Console.”
3) Decide if we mention streaming in Email 0 or push to Email 3.
4) Confirm we can send Email 3 (post-first-success) without confusing support load.
5) Ensure suppression rules for enterprise opps? (Probably not applicable for onboarding lifecycle, but confirm with Jordan/Stephanie if needed.)

Draft landing pages/docs alignment
- Docs PR (redwood-docs/pr-913) should use the same terms as the CTA.
- Quickstart PR (redwood-quickstarts/pr-412) needs to be the canonical “first request” referenced here.

Notes to self (Ben)
- The copy is still too long in Email 2 if we add troubleshooting. Keep it tight.
- If we keep “5 minutes” claim, make sure snippet really is minimal and doesn’t require extra SDK setup.

source.title: Benchmark Report Launch — Lifecycle Email Copy (v1)
source.content:
Working doc — v1 copy for benchmark report lifecycle emails.

Audience + goal
- PLG / self-serve: drive traffic to interactive benchmark page → signup / generate API key → first call within 24h.
- Enterprise leads (Dedicated/Private): drive landing page engagement → request a walkthrough / sizing call → book meeting.

Notes (claims / legal)
- Avoid absolute superlatives (e.g., “fastest”, “best”, “#1”).
- Use “in our tests”, “measured under the methodology described here”, “results vary by workload, region, and configuration”.
- If mentioning competitor baselines: only if approved in final report; otherwise keep it Redwood-only and methodology-forward.
- Always include a short disclaimer line + link to methodology summary on landing page.

Primary assets to link
- Interactive benchmark page (primary): https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
- PDF report (secondary): https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/report.pdf
- Methodology (supporting): https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar#methodology

Tracking / UTM conventions (Marketing Ops)
- utm_campaign=benchmark_report_launch
- utm_source=lifecycle
- utm_medium=email
- utm_content={variant_id}
- utm_term={segment}

Segments (initial)
A) PLG — active (had API key OR made ≥1 call in last 30d)
B) PLG — signed up, no activation (no successful completion / no tokens)
C) PLG — churned/inactive (no calls last 60–90d)
D) Enterprise leads — known (sales-owned, security/SLA signals)
E) Enterprise leads — net new (webinar registrants / report downloaders)

Send cadence (proposal)
- Launch Day: Email 1 (announcement)
- +3 days: Email 2 (methodology + how to interpret)
- +10 days: Email 3 (use-case angle / Dedicated & Private CTA)
- Triggered follow-ups:
  - If clicked “methodology” but no CTA click → send “How to reproduce / run your own workload” email
  - If exported CSV / clicked “Request demo” → immediate Sales/SE follow-up sequence

========================================
EMAIL 1 (Launch Day) — PLG (Active)
========================================
Variant A (benefit-forward)
Subject A1: New: our latency/$ benchmark report for production LLM inference
Subject A2: Benchmark: latency + cost across models & regions (interactive)
Preheader: Explore p50/p95, throughput, and $/1M tokens—plus the full methodology.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

We just published Redwood’s latest benchmark report on production inference economics: latency and cost efficiency (latency/$) across a curated set of models and regions.

What you’ll find:
- Interactive results by model + region (with filters)
- Supporting metrics (p50/p95 latency, throughput, $/1M tokens)
- A full “how we measured” methodology, configs, and caveats

CTA (primary): View the interactive benchmark →
CTA (secondary): Download the PDF report →

If you want, reply with your workload details (typical input/output tokens, concurrency, region) and we’ll point you to the closest matching view.

Disclaimer line (footer): Results are from Redwood tests under the published methodology; performance varies by workload, region, and configuration.

Variant B (credibility-forward)
Subject B1: How we measure production LLM performance (and what we found)
Subject B2: A reproducible benchmark for latency/$ (with configs)
Preheader: Benchmarks you can audit: methodology, stability checks, and regional sweeps.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Benchmarks are easy to debate. So we published ours with a reproducible methodology: workload definitions, prompt sets, concurrency levels, stability gates, and region sweeps.

Explore the results, then dig into the methodology to see exactly how numbers were produced.

CTA (primary): See results + methodology →
CTA (secondary): Download PDF →

Disclaimer line (footer): Results are from Redwood tests under the published methodology; results vary with workload and environment.

A/B test notes (Email 1 — PLG Active)
- Test goal: click-through to interactive page + downstream activation lift.
- Hypothesis: “benefit-forward” subject will drive higher opens; “credibility-forward” will drive higher quality clicks.
- Success metrics: Open rate, CTR, % filter interactions, % CTA clicks (Get API key / Docs), activation within 24h.

========================================
EMAIL 1 (Launch Day) — PLG (Signed up, no activation)
========================================
Variant A (activation CTA)
Subject A1: Benchmark results + a 5-minute quickstart to try it yourself
Subject A2: Want to test your workload? Start with our benchmark baseline
Preheader: Use the same model/region views from the report—then run your first request.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

We released a new benchmark report on latency/$ across models and regions—plus an interactive table you can filter to match your workload.

If you haven’t tried Redwood yet, the fastest path is:
1) Pick a model + region from the benchmark page
2) Generate an API key
3) Run the quickstart (streaming supported)

CTA (primary): Open benchmark + quickstart →
CTA (secondary): View methodology →

Disclaimer line (footer): Benchmark results are measured under our published methodology; results vary by workload and configuration.

Variant B (pain-point CTA)
Subject B1: Lower latency is great—predictable latency is better (p95 included)
Subject B2: See p95 latency by region (and then try it)
Preheader: Interactive benchmark results include p95 latency and cost efficiency.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

If you’re building something user-facing, p95 latency matters as much as headline throughput.

Our new benchmark report includes p50/p95 latency and cost efficiency (latency/$) across models and regions—plus the methodology so you can interpret results correctly.

CTA (primary): View p95 results →
CTA (secondary): Get started with Redwood →

========================================
EMAIL 1 (Launch Day) — PLG (Inactive / churned)
========================================
Variant A (what changed)
Subject A1: What’s new at Redwood: updated benchmark results by region
Subject A2: Updated latency/$ benchmarks (interactive + PDF)
Preheader: New runs, updated methodology, and an easier way to compare by region.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

We’ve made major improvements in how we measure and publish production inference performance: region sweeps, stability checks, and a single view for latency + cost efficiency.

If you haven’t looked at Redwood in a while, here’s the new benchmark report + interactive page:

CTA (primary): View the benchmark report →
CTA (secondary): See methodology →

========================================
EMAIL 1 (Launch Day) — Enterprise leads (Sales-owned)
========================================
Variant A (Dedicated/Private positioning)
Subject A1: New benchmark report: latency/$ by model + region (plus methodology)
Subject A2: Production inference economics: interactive benchmark results
Preheader: Includes p95 latency, region sweeps, and a reproducible methodology.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

We published Redwood’s benchmark report on production inference economics: latency and cost efficiency (latency/$) across models and regions.

Why it matters for teams evaluating Dedicated or Private deployments:
- Regional consistency (measured across sweeps)
- p95 latency and stability notes (not just averages)
- Clear methodology and caveats you can review with your engineering team

CTA (primary): View interactive benchmark →
CTA (secondary): Book a 20-min walkthrough (Dedicated/Private) →

If you share your target region(s), SLO (p95), and concurrency, we can map you to the closest benchmark slice and recommend a PoC plan.

Disclaimer line (footer): Results are from Redwood tests under the published methodology; performance varies by workload, region, and configuration.

Variant B (security/review-friendly)
Subject B1: Benchmark report with methodology + data retention notes
Subject B2: Benchmark results you can audit (methodology included)
Preheader: Designed for engineering review—configs, caveats, and reproducibility.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

We know benchmark claims often become a security/architecture review topic. This report includes the “how we measured” methodology and supporting notes so your team can audit what’s included and what’s not.

CTA (primary): View benchmark + methodology →
CTA (secondary): Request a technical deep dive →

========================================
EMAIL 2 (+3 days) — All segments (Methodology explainer)
========================================
Subject options
- The methodology behind our latency/$ benchmark (and how to interpret it)
- How to compare latency + cost across regions (without getting misled)

Preheader: Workloads, prompt sets, concurrency, warmup, caching policy, and stability checks.

Body (shared; swap CTA per segment):
Hi {{first_name}},

A quick guide to interpreting the benchmark report:
- Workloads: We test across defined prefill/decode mixes to reflect common production patterns.
- Concurrency: Runs are executed at fixed concurrency levels with warmup.
- Caching/batching: The methodology explicitly states what is enabled and what’s held constant.
- Stability gates: We rerun when variance exceeds thresholds and report supporting metrics.
- Metrics: latency/$ is a normalized metric; we also show p50/p95 latency, throughput, and $/1M tokens.

CTA mapping:
- PLG active/no activation: “Open interactive benchmark” / “Run quickstart”
- Enterprise: “Book walkthrough” / “Request sizing”

Footer disclaimer: Results are from Redwood tests under the published methodology; your results may differ.

========================================
EMAIL 3 (+10 days) — Enterprise-focused (Dedicated/Private CTA)
========================================
Subject options
- From benchmark to production: how teams hit p95 SLOs
- Need predictable throughput? Dedicated capacity options + benchmark context

Preheader: If you’re scaling traffic or have data isolation needs, here’s how to think about deployment modes.

Body:
Hi {{first_name}},

The benchmark report is a baseline. The next step is aligning the deployment mode to your operational constraints:
- Hosted API: fastest iteration, managed scaling
- Dedicated: predictable throughput + isolation, SLA-backed
- Private (VPC/on-prem): customer-managed networking + compliance requirements

If you send:
- target regions
- expected concurrency
- typical input/output tokens
- p95 SLO
…we’ll recommend a PoC plan and the closest benchmark slices to anchor expectations.

CTA (primary): Book Dedicated/Private sizing call →
CTA (secondary): View benchmark methodology →

========================================
Operational details / TODOs
========================================
- Confirm final landing page URL and PDF URL (Wei / Docs).
- Confirm approved disclaimer phrasing and any prohibited terms (Michael / Sofia).
- Ensure analytics events fire on:
  - benchmark page visit from email
  - filter changes
  - export clicks
  - “Get API key” click
  - “Request demo” click
- Provide Sales with a consistent follow-up snippet (Markus + Stephanie/Lukas).

Questions / open items
- Do we include a small in-email chart thumbnail? (Potentially increases clicks; check rendering in dark mode.)
- Do we mention “latency/$” in subject lines for all segments or use “latency and cost” for broader clarity?
- What is the final segmentation for enterprise (security/compliance signal vs size vs region-specific)?

Copy blocks (for reuse)
- Short disclaimer: “Results are from Redwood tests under the published methodology; performance varies by workload, region, and configuration.”
- Credibility line: “Methodology includes prompt sets, concurrency levels, warmup, caching policy, stability gates, and region sweeps.”

text:
Working doc — v1 copy for benchmark report lifecycle emails.

Audience + goal
- PLG / self-serve: drive traffic to interactive benchmark page → signup / generate API key → first call within 24h.
- Enterprise leads (Dedicated/Private): drive landing page engagement → request a walkthrough / sizing call → book meeting.

Notes (claims / legal)
- Avoid absolute superlatives (e.g., “fastest”, “best”, “#1”).
- Use “in our tests”, “measured under the methodology described here”, “results vary by workload, region, and configuration”.
- If mentioning competitor baselines: only if approved in final report; otherwise keep it Redwood-only and methodology-forward.
- Always include a short disclaimer line + link to methodology summary on landing page.

Primary assets to link
- Interactive benchmark page (primary): https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
- PDF report (secondary): https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/report.pdf
- Methodology (supporting): https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar#methodology

Tracking / UTM conventions (Marketing Ops)
- utm_campaign=benchmark_report_launch
- utm_source=lifecycle
- utm_medium=email
- utm_content={variant_id}
- utm_term={segment}

source.title: Vertical ABM Pilot (Regulated Workloads) — Post‑Pilot Retro Notes (informal)
source.body:
Owner: Ben
Date: 2025-06-17 (compiled from notes across Weeks 1–8)
Audience: Marketing/Growth + PMM + Sales + SE + Security/Compliance + CS

Context / what this is
- This is an informal retro on the 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot targeting regulated workloads (initially FinServ + Healthcare).
- Focus was Dedicated + Private (VPC/on‑prem). We explicitly tried to avoid pulling in “net-new product,” except minor docs/enablement.
- Goal of this doc: capture what worked, what didn’t, where we got stuck (esp. security review), and what we should change before we scale.

Quick outcome snapshot (directionally, not a KPI report)
- FinServ produced the highest-quality conversations fastest; Healthcare had the most variability (PHI sensitivity + on‑prem/networking blockers).
- 2 meaningful pilots/POC tracks ran in parallel (NorthBridge, Helix). Both required heavy SE + Security involvement and “MAP”-style coordination.
- The “security CTA” (offer a security review + evidence pack index) converted better than “generic demo,” but only when we responded fast.
- Biggest constraint: turnaround time for security questionnaires + evidence sharing workflow + confusion around retention/residency language.

What worked (keep / double down)
1) The regulated talk track when it stayed concrete
- The best-performing messaging was specific and operational: predictable throughput, clear deployment modes (Dedicated vs Private), auditability (audit logs), access controls (SSO/SAML), encryption + KMS/HSM alignment, and retention controls.
- “Unified runtime across hosted/dedicated/private” landed well with Platform/ML Eng, but CISOs only cared after we proved auditability + governance.

2) FinServ persona mapping + discovery questions
- The FinServ persona framing (CISO/Security + Platform + Risk/Compliance + Procurement) was tighter and led to better multi-threading.
- Discovery questions that performed:
  - “Where does audit logging need to land (SIEM? S3? Splunk?) and what fields do you require?”
  - “Do you need VPC-only connectivity / PrivateLink, or full on‑prem/airgapped requirements?”
  - “What is your default retention policy for vendor systems? Are there exceptions for transient processing?”
  - “Who signs off on model/prompt changes (change control)?”

3) Evidence pack index as a ‘confidence unlock’
- The evidence pack index (even before sending the actual artifacts) reduced friction. Prospects liked that we had a defined process for sharing SOC2, diagrams, etc.
- Having Naomi/Olga’s “approved language” handy prevented accidental over-claims (we avoided “HIPAA-certified/ready” phrasing).

4) SE-led ‘pilot plan’ template
- Where we used a standard 2–3 week pilot plan (benchmark + architecture validation + security checklist), deals advanced faster.
- CS handoff checklist (SLOs, dashboards, runbook, access) helped closeout narrative and prevented post-pilot drift.

5) Partner motion (AWS Marketplace) helped late-stage mechanics
- The AWS Marketplace private offer workflow helped remove a procurement objection for a late-stage FinServ target.
- Prospects responded well to “you can buy through your existing marketplace process” as an operational simplifier.

What did NOT work (stop / change)
1) Outreach variability + inconsistent CTA
- We oscillated between CTAs (benchmark download vs security review call vs demo). This muddied attribution and made SDR follow-up inconsistent.
- Best-performing sequence variant was the one that offered: “15 min security scoping call + we’ll map Dedicated vs Private and provide an evidence pack index.”
- LinkedIn targeting was low volume. When it hit, it was expensive and required very tight job title filters.

2) Healthcare scope felt too broad / hard to qualify quickly
- “Healthcare” includes payer/provider/billing/telehealth; security + networking constraints vary wildly.
- Many conversations got stuck in the “PHI adjacency” gray zone and required legal/compliance nuance before we even had technical champions.
- We lacked crisp disqualifiers early (ex: “must support air-gapped on-prem in <30 days with customer-managed hardware” => not pilot-friendly).

3) Confusion around retention/residency wording
- Multiple threads got stuck on:
  - What data is stored where (prompt/response logs, metadata, audit logs)
  - Default retention vs configurable retention
  - Residency constraints by region / cloud
- We repeatedly re-explained this in bespoke emails. We need a single canonical, compliance-reviewed paragraph + diagram.

4) Security questionnaire bottlenecks (bandwidth + workflow)
- Biggest drag on speed:
  - Intake: who owns which questions (Security vs Product vs SE)
  - Turnaround: requests came in clustered (often after first technical meeting)
  - Sharing artifacts: redaction + controlled distribution took time
- When we took >5 business days to respond, we saw deals cool or the champion lose momentum.

5) Over-reliance on “we can do Private” without crisp packaging guardrails
- Some AEs/SDRs implied Private = instant compliance solution. This created expectation mismatches on:
  - timeline to stand up VPC/on‑prem
  - what is included vs add-ons (retention controls, extended audit log retention, support tiers)
  - who does what (Redwood vs customer) for networking + key mgmt

Content gaps (assets we should build / revise)
1) Regulated landing page improvements
- The landing page draft improved, but still needs:
  - a short “what we log / where it goes” section
  - a single “Dedicated vs Private: when to choose which” block
  - explicit language about controlled evidence sharing process (without making promises about instant SOC2 access)

2) “Security FAQ” needs an ‘answer-first’ format for Sales
- FAQ is good but too long to use live. Sales needs a 1–2 page “top 12 questions” quick sheet:
  - SOC2 scope (what systems, what period) and how we share
  - encryption at rest/in transit + KMS options
  - audit log schema + export patterns
  - SSO/SAML/SCIM support (and known edge cases)
  - retention configuration boundaries

3) Technical reference: audit log export example (already started)
- The example PR was helpful, but we should package it into a “Regulated Quickstart: Private (VPC)” doc with:
  - architecture diagram
  - example config
  - log export sample payload
  - SIEM integration pointers

4) Better healthcare-specific one-pager
- The healthcare one-pager needs cleaner guardrails:
  - “PHI-adjacent vs PHI-handling” clarity
  - recommended deployment patterns + responsibilities
  - disqualifiers (e.g., truly air-gapped on-prem with no managed connectivity)

Process + operations gaps
1) Security intake + SLA
- Recommendation: create a single intake form + alias that auto-creates a lightweight ticket and assigns an owner (Olga as triage; Naomi as escalation).
- Set internal SLA target for ABM pilot accounts:
  - 2 business days for “first response”
  - 5 business days for “substantive response/evidence plan”
- We should define what “substantive response” means (even if evidence sharing takes longer).

2) Evidence sharing workflow
- We need a standard “evidence pack request → NDA check → redaction → share → expiry/revoke” workflow.
- Today this is mostly tribal knowledge and email threads.

3) Pilot credits/discount policy needs a single blurb
- We wasted cycles in Slack clarifying guardrails. A one-paragraph internal policy + approved prospect-facing language would reduce confusion.

4) CS handoff timing
- For regulated pilots, CS/TAM involvement should start at pilot kickoff (not after week 2) to ensure:
  - monitoring/SLO reporting is ready
  - closeout deck is consistent
  - the “security posture narrative” continues into expansion

Recommendations for next iteration (specific)
A) Vertical focus: choose FinServ first; narrow Healthcare to one sub-vertical
- FinServ: keep as primary vertical for next ABM wave.
- Healthcare: if we keep, narrow to 1 of:
  - medical billing / revenue cycle (less PHI sensitivity, clearer ROI)
  - payer member support (often more ‘PHI-adjacent’ with strong governance)
- Avoid broad “provider / clinical summarization” positioning until we have tighter PHI guardrails + stronger proof points.

B) CTA + offer: standardize around “Security + Architecture Review”
- Primary CTA: 20–30 min “Regulated Readiness” call (Security + SE optional) with a checklist outcome.
- Secondary CTA: benchmark/pilot plan (only after readiness call).

C) Partner motion: formalize AWS Marketplace + 1–2 compliance partners
- Keep AWS Marketplace private offer playbook in the sequence for late-stage targets.
- Add co-sell targets earlier (tag accounts up front) so the partner team can activate intros.
- Explore 1–2 compliance/identity partners for joint webinars (SSO/SIEM story).

D) Asset refresh priorities (2-week sprint)
1) One canonical “Dedicated vs Private” positioning page (internal + external excerpt)
2) Security FAQ “top questions” quick sheet
3) Audit logs + retention diagram + a short, compliance-reviewed paragraph
4) Regulated pilot plan template (2–3 weeks) with responsibilities spelled out

E) Measurement: tighten attribution and quality signals
- Track stage progression by persona coverage (did we get Security + Platform + Procurement engaged?).
- Add a qualitative “security friction score” per account:
  - 1 = standard SOC2 request, light questionnaire
  - 2 = deeper audit log + retention questions
  - 3 = heavy custom terms / residency requirements / multiple evidence artifacts
- This will help forecast SE/Security bandwidth needed.

Loose ends / parking lot
- Need clarity on SAML/SCIM edge cases surfaced in at least one pilot (see support ticket). Sales needs a safe way to answer: “Supported; here are known constraints and typical resolution path.”
- On-prem packaging: we should create a lightweight “on-prem readiness checklist” so we don’t waste cycles with impossible timelines.
- Proof points: we need at least 1–2 anonymized “regulated workload” mini case studies (even if genericized) that include: timeline, deployment mode, security artifacts shared, and performance outcomes.

Action items (proposed owners)
- Security intake + SLA proposal: Olga + Naomi (draft), review with Dr. Aisha Rahman
- Dedicated vs Private positioning page: Soojin + Lauren + Vivek (with SE review)
- Regulated landing page edits + publish: Elliot + Kira + Ethan (Security review)
- Pilot plan template + CS handoff: Markus + Rachel + Liam
- AWS Marketplace playbook (internal): Partnerships + Sofia/Michael for standard terms guidance

Notes to self (Ben)
- The pilot proved there’s real demand, but our limiting reagent is speed + consistency in security responses.
- If we scale without fixing intake/evidence workflow, we’ll just create more stalled “interested but blocked” accounts.
- Don’t broaden verticals until we have a repeatable ‘security review → pilot → procurement’ lane that doesn’t burn out Security/SE.

title: Vertical ABM Pilot (Regulated Workloads) — Post‑Pilot Retro Notes (informal)
preview:
Owner: Ben
Date: 2025-06-17 (compiled from notes across Weeks 1–8)
Audience: Marketing/Growth + PMM + Sales + SE + Security/Compliance + CS

Context / what this is
- This is an informal retro on the 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot targeting regulated workloads (initially FinServ + Healthcare).
- Focus was Dedicated + Private (VPC/on‑prem). We explicitly tried to avoid pulling in “net-new product,” except minor docs/enablement.
- Goal of this doc: capture what worked, what didn’t, where we got stuck (esp. security review), and what we should change before we scale.

Quick outcome snapshot (directionally, not a KPI report)
- FinServ produced the highest-quality conversations fastest; Healthcare had the most variability (PHI sensitivity + on‑prem/networking blockers).
- 2 meaningful pilots/POC tracks ran in parallel (NorthBridge, Helix). Both required heavy SE + Security involvement and “MAP”-style coordination.
- The “security CTA” (offer a security review + evidence pack index) converted better than “generic demo,” but only when we responded fast.
- Biggest constraint: turnaround time for security questionnaires + evidence sharing workflow + confusion around retention/residency language.

What worked (keep / double down)
1) The regulated talk track when it stayed concrete
- The best-performing messaging was specific and operational: predictable throughput, clear deployment modes (Dedicated vs Private), auditability (audit logs), access controls (SSO/SAML), encryption + KMS/HSM alignment, and retention controls.
- “Unified runtime across hosted/dedicated/private” landed well with Platform/ML Eng, but CISOs only cared after we proved auditability + governance.

2) FinServ persona mapping + discovery questions
- The FinServ persona framing (CISO/Security + Platform + Risk/Compliance + Procurement) was tighter and led to better multi-threading.
- Discovery questions that performed:
  - “Where does audit logging need to land (SIEM? S3? Splunk?) and what fields do you require?”
  - “Do you need VPC-only connectivity / PrivateLink, or full on‑prem/airgapped requirements?”
  - “What is your default retention policy for vendor systems? Are there exceptions for transient processing?”
  - “Who signs off on model/prompt changes (change control)?”

3) Evidence pack index as a ‘confidence unlock’
- The evidence pack index (even before sending the actual artifacts) reduced friction. Prospects liked that we had a defined process for sharing SOC2, diagrams, etc.
- Having Naomi/Olga’s “approved language” handy prevented accidental over-claims (we avoided “HIPAA-certified/ready” phrasing).

4) SE-led ‘pilot plan’ template
- Where we used a standard 2–3 week pilot plan (benchmark + architecture validation + security checklist), deals advanced faster.
- CS handoff checklist (SLOs, dashboards, runbook, access) helped closeout narrative and prevented post-pilot drift.

5) Partner motion (AWS Marketplace) helped late-stage mechanics
- The AWS Marketplace private offer workflow helped remove a procurement objection for a late-stage FinServ target.
- Prospects responded well to “you can buy through your existing marketplace process” as an operational simplifier.

What did NOT work (stop / change)
1) Outreach variability + inconsistent CTA
- We oscillated between CTAs (benchmark download vs security review call vs demo). This muddied attribution and made SDR follow-up inconsistent.
- Best-performing sequence variant was the one that offered: “15 min security scoping call + we’ll map Dedicated vs Private and provide an evidence pack index.”
- LinkedIn targeting was low volume. When it hit, it was expensive and required very tight job title filters.

2) Healthcare scope felt too broad / hard to qualify quickly
- “Healthcare” includes payer/provider/billing/telehealth; security + networking constraints vary wildly.
- Many conversations got stuck in the “PHI adjacency” gray zone and required legal/compliance nuance before we even had technical champions.
- We lacked crisp disqualifiers early (ex: “must support air-gapped on-prem in <30 days with customer-managed hardware” => not pilot-friendly).

3) Confusion around retention/residency wording
- Multiple threads got stuck on:
  - What data is stored where (prompt/response logs, metadata, audit logs)
  - Default retention vs configurable retention
  - Residency constraints by region / cloud
- We repeatedly re-explained this in bespoke emails. We need a single canonical, compliance-reviewed paragraph + diagram.

4) Security questionnaire bottlenecks (bandwidth + workflow)
- Biggest drag on speed:
  - Intake: who owns which questions (Security vs Product vs SE)
  - Turnaround: requests came in clustered (often after first technical meeting)
  - Sharing artifacts: redaction + controlled distribution took time
- When we took >5 business days to respond, we saw deals cool or the champion lose momentum.

5) Over-reliance on “we can do Private” without crisp packaging guardrails
- Some AEs/SDRs implied Private = instant compliance solution. This created expectation mismatches on:
  - timeline to stand up VPC/on‑prem
  - what is included vs add-ons (retention controls, extended audit log retention, support tiers)
  - who does what (Redwood vs customer) for networking + key mgmt

Content gaps (assets we should build / revise)
1) Regulated landing page improvements
- The landing page draft improved, but still needs:
  - a short “what we log / where it goes” section
  - a single “Dedicated vs Private: when to choose which” block
  - explicit language about controlled evidence sharing process (without making promises about instant SOC2 access)

2) “Security FAQ” needs an ‘answer-first’ format for Sales
- FAQ is good but too long to use live. Sales needs a 1–2 page “top 12 questions” quick sheet:
  - SOC2 scope (what systems, what period) and how we share
  - encryption at rest/in transit + KMS options
  - audit log schema + export patterns
  - SSO/SAML/SCIM support (and known edge cases)
  - retention configuration boundaries

3) Technical reference: audit log export example (already started)
- The example PR was helpful, but we should package it into a “Regulated Quickstart: Private (VPC)” doc with:
  - architecture diagram
  - example config
  - log export sample payload
  - SIEM integration pointers

4) Better healthcare-specific one-pager
- The healthcare one-pager needs cleaner guardrails:
  - “PHI-adjacent vs PHI-handling” clarity
  - recommended deployment patterns + responsibilities
  - disqualifiers (e.g., truly air-gapped on-prem with no managed connectivity)

Process + operations gaps
1) Security intake + SLA
- Recommendation: create a single intake form + alias that auto-creates a lightweight ticket and assigns an owner (Olga as triage; Naomi as escalation).
- Set internal SLA target for ABM pilot accounts:
  - 2 business days for “first response”
  - 5 business days for “substantive response/evidence plan”
- We should define what “substantive response” means (even if evidence sharing takes longer).

2) Evidence sharing workflow
- We need a standard “evidence pack request → NDA check → redaction → share → expiry/revoke” workflow.
- Today this is mostly tribal knowledge and email threads.

3) Pilot credits/discount policy needs a single blurb
- We wasted cycles in Slack clarifying guardrails. A one-paragraph internal policy + approved prospect-facing language would reduce confusion.

4) CS handoff timing
- For regulated pilots, CS/TAM involvement should start at pilot kickoff (not after week 2) to ensure:
  - monitoring/SLO reporting is ready
  - closeout deck is consistent
  - the “security posture narrative” continues into expansion

Recommendations for next iteration (specific)
A) Vertical focus: choose FinServ first; narrow Healthcare to one sub-vertical
- FinServ: keep as primary vertical for next ABM wave.
- Healthcare: if we keep, narrow to 1 of:
  - medical billing / revenue cycle (less PHI sensitivity, clearer ROI)
  - payer member support (often more ‘PHI-adjacent’ with strong governance)
- Avoid broad “provider / clinical summarization” positioning until we have tighter PHI guardrails + stronger proof points.

B) CTA + offer: standardize around “Security + Architecture Review”
- Primary CTA: 20–30 min “Regulated Readiness” call (Security + SE optional) with a checklist outcome.
- Secondary CTA: benchmark/pilot plan (only after readiness call).

C) Partner motion: formalize AWS Marketplace + 1–2 compliance partners
- Keep AWS Marketplace private offer playbook in the sequence for late-stage targets.
- Add co-sell targets earlier (tag accounts up front) so the partner team can activate intros.
- Explore 1–2 compliance/identity partners for joint webinars (SSO/SIEM story).

D) Asset refresh priorities (2-week sprint)
1) One canonical “Dedicated vs Private” positioning page (internal + external excerpt)
2) Security FAQ “top questions” quick sheet
3) Audit logs + retention diagram + a short, compliance-reviewed paragraph
4) Regulated pilot plan template (2–3 weeks) with responsibilities spelled out

E) Measurement: tighten attribution and quality signals
- Track stage progression by persona coverage (did we get Security + Platform + Procurement engaged?).
- Add a qualitative “security friction score” per account:
  - 1 = standard SOC2 request, light questionnaire
  - 2 = deeper audit log + retention questions
  - 3 = heavy custom terms / residency requirements / multiple evidence artifacts
- This will help forecast SE/Security bandwidth needed.

Loose ends / parking lot
- Need clarity on SAML/SCIM edge cases surfaced in at least one pilot (see support ticket). Sales needs a safe way to answer: “Supported; here are known constraints and typical resolution path.”
- On-prem packaging: we should create a lightweight “on-prem readiness checklist” so we don’t waste cycles with impossible timelines.
- Proof points: we need at least 1–2 anonymized “regulated workload” mini case studies (even if genericized) that include: timeline, deployment mode, security artifacts shared, and performance outcomes.

Action items (proposed owners)
- Security intake + SLA proposal: Olga + Naomi (draft), review with Dr. Aisha Rahman
- Dedicated vs Private positioning page: Soojin + Lauren + Vivek (with SE review)
- Regulated landing page edits + publish: Elliot + Kira + Ethan (Security review)
- Pilot plan template + CS handoff: Markus + Rachel + Liam
- AWS Marketplace playbook (internal): Partnerships + Sofia/Michael for standard terms guidance

Notes to self (Ben)
- The pilot proved there’s real demand, but our limiting reagent is speed + consistency in security responses.
- If we scale without fixing intake/evidence workflow, we’ll just create more stalled “interested but blocked” accounts.
- Don’t broaden verticals until we have a repeatable ‘security review → pilot → procurement’ lane that doesn’t burn out Security/SE.

source.title: Webinar Outline (Draft): Running Regulated LLM Workloads with Predictable Performance + Auditability
source.body:
Working title options (PMM to finalize)
1) Regulated GenAI in Production: Low Latency, Audit Trails, and Deployment Control
2) From POC to Production: Serving LLMs for Regulated Workloads (FinServ + Healthcare)
3) Auditable LLM Inference: Dedicated + Private Deployments without the Ops Tax

Audience + positioning
- Primary audience: Security leaders (CISO/Director of Security), Platform Engineering, ML Engineering, and technical product owners who are tasked with enabling GenAI while meeting auditability/residency/retention requirements.
- Secondary audience: Procurement / vendor risk management (VRM) stakeholders who influence approvals.
- Key message: Redwood gives teams a single inference platform across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments, with production-grade performance economics and security/compliance controls required for regulated environments.

Compliance-safe language (do/don’t)
- DO say:
  - “Supports customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM integrations available in Private deployments)”
  - “Audit logging available; export patterns supported”
  - “Configurable retention and data handling options depending on deployment mode and contract”
  - “We can share SOC 2 materials under NDA via our evidence pack process”
  - “Private (VPC/on-prem) options for data isolation and residency needs”
- DO NOT say:
  - “HIPAA certified” / “HIPAA compliant out of the box”
  - “Guaranteed residency in every region” (must be scoped per customer)
  - “We never store anything” (retention depends on deployment + settings)
  - Any claim implying attestation beyond current evidence

Goal + success criteria
- Goal: Drive qualified conversations and security reviews for Dedicated + Private deployments.
- Success metrics (Ops to instrument):
  - Registrations: 150 target / 75 minimum
  - Attendance rate: 35–45%
  - Meeting conversion: 10–15 meetings booked within 2 weeks post-event
  - Evidence pack requests: 10+ (tracked as high-intent signal)
  - 2–4 POC discussions started (qualified for Dedicated/Private)

Format
- Virtual event (Zoom / Webinar platform)
- 45 minutes live + 15 minutes optional Q&A (or hard stop at 60)
- Recording gated post-event for ABM follow-up

Proposed date/time (needs speaker confirmation)
- Option A: Wed 2025-06-18 @ 10:00am PT / 1:00pm ET
- Option B: Thu 2025-06-20 @ 8:00am PT / 11:00am ET

Speakers + roles (draft)
- Host / MC: Camila Reyes (VP Marketing)
- Product + demo: Markus Klein (Solutions Engineering Lead)
- Security + compliance: Dr. Aisha Rahman (CISO) + Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance Product Alignment) (Aisha for “why it matters”, Naomi for specifics + evidence process)
- Optional guest (if we can secure in time): Partner speaker from AWS Marketplace team (process + private offers) OR an anonymized customer story read-out (no logo unless approved)

Run of show (minute-by-minute)
0:00–0:03 | Welcome + agenda (Camila)
- Quick framing: regulated buyers face dual constraints (security/compliance + cost/latency expectations).
- Housekeeping: Q&A via chat, recording, how to request evidence pack.

0:03–0:10 | What “regulated workloads” mean in practice (Soojin or Markus)
- Common requirements we see:
  - Data isolation (VPC/on-prem), identity controls (SSO/SAML), audit logs
  - Encryption + key management patterns
  - Retention controls, residency considerations
  - Procurement/VRM expectations: SOC 2, security questionnaires, incident response commitments
- Typical failure mode: teams choose an endpoint that works for a demo, then hit compliance + ops walls during rollout.

0:10–0:20 | Reference architectures (Markus)
- Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private: when each is a fit
  - Hosted: fastest start; good for non-sensitive or de-identified workloads
  - Dedicated: predictable throughput + isolation, Redwood-managed
  - Private: customer network boundary (VPC/on-prem), deeper controls
- What stays consistent across modes: API surface, operational tooling, rollout strategies.

0:20–0:35 | Live demo flow (Markus + Fatima as backup)
Demo objective: show “auditable, controllable inference” end-to-end (not just a model prompt).

Demo prerequisites
- Pre-record a backup demo (video) in case of live issues.
- Use a synthetic dataset (no PHI/PCI/PII).
- Ensure screenshots are scrubbed.

Demo storyline (suggested)
1) Start with an app view (lightweight) triggering inference requests
- Show streaming response + structured output (briefly)
- Mention predictable latency/throughput as a platform concern (not a single-model claim)

2) Show Redwood Console: request tracing + cost/latency breakdown
- Per-route/model: p50/p95 latency
- Token-level cost visibility
- Error analysis (one example)

3) Show operational controls: routing + fallback policy
- Example: policy routes regulated traffic to Private deployment; non-sensitive to Hosted/Dedicated (if applicable)
- Example: automated fallback to compatible model variant when capacity constrained (emphasize “policy-driven”)

4) Security/controls: audit logs + export pattern (high-level)
- What gets logged (examples): timestamp, actor/service identity, request metadata (no sensitive payload), action/event type, resource identifiers
- Mention export options depend on deployment and customer environment; show a sample export (JSON lines) from a non-sensitive environment

5) Close demo with “what a pilot looks like”
- 2–3 week architecture + benchmark validation
- Success criteria: SLOs, integration checks, security review completion, and decision checkpoint

0:35–0:47 | Security & compliance deep dive (Dr. Aisha + Naomi)
- How we handle common security review areas:
  - SOC 2: how to request report + what’s covered (share under NDA)
  - Encryption in transit and at rest (deployment-specific details); customer-managed keys supported in Private
  - Identity: RBAC + SSO/SAML (note SCIM where applicable; confirm for prospect specifics)
  - Audit logs: purpose, fields, retention options; evidence pack includes sample artifacts
  - Data retention + residency: controlled via contract + configuration; must be confirmed per customer
  - Incident response: process overview + response commitments (no hard numbers unless in SLA)
- “How to get answers fast”: evidence pack intake + questionnaire turnaround expectations

0:47–0:55 | Audience Q&A (All)
- Moderator (Camila) reads questions; Naomi handles security specifics; Markus handles technical.

0:55–1:00 | Close + CTA (Camila)
- Primary CTA: “Book a 30-min security & architecture review”
- Secondary CTA: “Request the regulated evidence pack (under NDA)”
- Optional CTA: “Ask for a pilot plan template / benchmark checklist”

Audience Q&A prompts (seed questions for the moderator)
Security / compliance
- What exactly is included in your SOC 2 scope and how do we obtain the report?
- Do you support customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM) and what’s required from our side?
- What do your audit logs capture, and can we export them to our SIEM?
- Can we configure retention for prompts/outputs/metadata? What are default retention assumptions?
- What identity options do you support (SAML/SSO, RBAC, SCIM)? Any known edge cases?

Architecture / operations
- What’s the practical difference between Dedicated and Private for regulated workloads?
- How do rollouts/canaries work when we need strict change control?
- How do you handle failover/region isolation for regulated traffic?
- What does a typical onboarding look like for VPC deployments?

Performance / economics
- How should we think about cost controls without sacrificing latency?
- What workload patterns benefit most from batching and caching?
- What metrics do you recommend we track for production (p95, error rate, token cost)?

Procurement / rollout
- How long does a typical security review + procurement cycle take?
- Do you offer an AWS Marketplace private offer path?

Promotion plan (GTM)
Targeting
- Primary: VP/Director Security, CISO staff, Head of Platform Eng, ML Platform Lead, Principal/Staff Eng in data/AI platform
- Secondary: Compliance, VRM, procurement (as optional invite)

Channels
1) Email (ABM list + house list)
- 2 invitation waves + 2 reminders + last-day reminder
- Add a “request evidence pack” CTA in confirmation email

2) LinkedIn
- Organic posts from speakers (Camila, Markus, Aisha)
- Paid: job title targeting (note low volume; focus on NA + UK + DACH)
- Creative: “Audit-ready inference” / “VPC + audit logs + predictable throughput”

3) Partner channel
- AWS co-sell: invite targeted accounts flagged for Marketplace motion
- Optional: invite security consultants / GRC partners (if we have a list)

4) On-site
- Banner on regulated workloads landing page
- Exit-intent modal only for regulated page traffic (avoid site-wide spam)

Creative + copy notes
- Keep claims scoped; include footnote: “Security capabilities vary by deployment mode; details provided during security review.”

Operational checklist
- Landing page: event registration + calendar invites + UTM tracking (Tyler)
- Speaker prep: 30-min dry run; Q&A run-through; compliance review of slides (Soojin + Naomi + Elliot)
- Demo: run in staging; ensure no customer data; record backup
- Lead routing: registrants matched to target accounts; assign AE owner; book meeting CTA routes to scheduler

Follow-up plan (post-webinar)
Timeline
- T+0 hours: “Thanks + recording + slides (if approved)” email

title: Webinar Outline (Draft): Running Regulated LLM Workloads with Predictable Performance + Auditability
preview:
Working title options (PMM to finalize)
1) Regulated GenAI in Production: Low Latency, Audit Trails, and Deployment Control
2) From POC to Production: Serving LLMs for Regulated Workloads (FinServ + Healthcare)
3) Auditable LLM Inference: Dedicated + Private Deployments without the Ops Tax

Audience + positioning
- Primary audience: Security leaders (CISO/Director of Security), Platform Engineering, ML Engineering, and technical product owners who are tasked with enabling GenAI while meeting auditability/residency/retention requirements.
- Secondary audience: Procurement / vendor risk management (VRM) stakeholders who influence approvals.
- Key message: Redwood gives teams a single inference platform across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments, with production-grade performance economics and security/compliance controls required for regulated environments.

Compliance-safe language (do/don’t)
- DO say:
  - “Supports customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM integrations available in Private deployments)”
  - “Audit logging available; export patterns supported”
  - “Configurable retention and data handling options depending on deployment mode and contract”
  - “We can share SOC 2 materials under NDA via our evidence pack process”
  - “Private (VPC/on-prem) options for data isolation and residency needs”
- DO NOT say:
  - “HIPAA certified” / “HIPAA compliant out of the box”
  - “Guaranteed residency in every region” (must be scoped per customer)
  - “We never store anything” (retention depends on deployment + settings)
  - Any claim implying attestation beyond current evidence

Goal + success criteria
- Goal: Drive qualified conversations and security reviews for Dedicated + Private deployments.
- Success metrics (Ops to instrument):
  - Registrations: 150 target / 75 minimum
  - Attendance rate: 35–45%
  - Meeting conversion: 10–15 meetings booked within 2 weeks post-event
  - Evidence pack requests: 10+ (tracked as high-intent signal)
  - 2–4 POC discussions started (qualified for Dedicated/Private)

Format
- Virtual event (Zoom / Webinar platform)
- 45 minutes live + 15 minutes optional Q&A (or hard stop at 60)
- Recording gated post-event for ABM follow-up

Proposed date/time (needs speaker confirmation)
- Option A: Wed 2025-06-18 @ 10:00am PT / 1:00pm ET
- Option B: Thu 2025-06-20 @ 8:00am PT / 11:00am ET

Speakers + roles (draft)
- Host / MC: Camila Reyes (VP Marketing)
- Product + demo: Markus Klein (Solutions Engineering Lead)
- Security + compliance: Dr. Aisha Rahman (CISO) + Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance Product Alignment) (Aisha for “why it matters”, Naomi for specifics + evidence process)
- Optional guest (if we can secure in time): Partner speaker from AWS Marketplace team (process + private offers) OR an anonymized customer story read-out (no logo unless approved)

Run of show (minute-by-minute)
0:00–0:03 | Welcome + agenda (Camila)
- Quick framing: regulated buyers face dual constraints (security/compliance + cost/latency expectations).
- Housekeeping: Q&A via chat, recording, how to request evidence pack.

0:03–0:10 | What “regulated workloads” mean in practice (Soojin or Markus)
- Common requirements we see:
  - Data isolation (VPC/on-prem), identity controls (SSO/SAML), audit logs
  - Encryption + key management patterns
  - Retention controls, residency considerations
  - Procurement/VRM expectations: SOC 2, security questionnaires, incident response commitments
- Typical failure mode: teams choose an endpoint that works for a demo, then hit compliance + ops walls during rollout.

0:10–0:20 | Reference architectures (Markus)
- Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private: when each is a fit
  - Hosted: fastest start; good for non-sensitive or de-identified workloads
  - Dedicated: predictable throughput + isolation, Redwood-managed
  - Private: customer network boundary (VPC/on-prem), deeper controls
- What stays consistent across modes: API surface, operational tooling, rollout strategies.

0:20–0:35 | Live demo flow (Markus + Fatima as backup)
Demo objective: show “auditable, controllable inference” end-to-end (not just a model prompt).

Demo prerequisites
- Pre-record a backup demo (video) in case of live issues.
- Use a synthetic dataset (no PHI/PCI/PII).
- Ensure screenshots are scrubbed.

Demo storyline (suggested)
1) Start with an app view (lightweight) triggering inference requests
- Show streaming response + structured output (briefly)
- Mention predictable latency/throughput as a platform concern (not a single-model claim)

2) Show Redwood Console: request tracing + cost/latency breakdown
- Per-route/model: p50/p95 latency
- Token-level cost visibility
- Error analysis (one example)

3) Show operational controls: routing + fallback policy
- Example: policy routes regulated traffic to Private deployment; non-sensitive to Hosted/Dedicated (if applicable)
- Example: automated fallback to compatible model variant when capacity constrained (emphasize “policy-driven”)

4) Security/controls: audit logs + export pattern (high-level)
- What gets logged (examples): timestamp, actor/service identity, request metadata (no sensitive payload), action/event type, resource identifiers
- Mention export options depend on deployment and customer environment; show a sample export (JSON lines) from a non-sensitive environment

5) Close demo with “what a pilot looks like”
- 2–3 week architecture + benchmark validation
- Success criteria: SLOs, integration checks, security review completion, and decision checkpoint

0:35–0:47 | Security & compliance deep dive (Dr. Aisha + Naomi)
- How we handle common security review areas:
  - SOC 2: how to request report + what’s covered (share under NDA)
  - Encryption in transit and at rest (deployment-specific details); customer-managed keys supported in Private
  - Identity: RBAC + SSO/SAML (note SCIM where applicable; confirm for prospect specifics)
  - Audit logs: purpose, fields, retention options; evidence pack includes sample artifacts
  - Data retention + residency: controlled via contract + configuration; must be confirmed per customer
  - Incident response: process overview + response commitments (no hard numbers unless in SLA)
- “How to get answers fast”: evidence pack intake + questionnaire turnaround expectations

0:47–0:55 | Audience Q&A (All)
- Moderator (Camila) reads questions; Naomi handles security specifics; Markus handles technical.

0:55–1:00 | Close + CTA (Camila)
- Primary CTA: “Book a 30-min security & architecture review”
- Secondary CTA: “Request the regulated evidence pack (under NDA)”
- Optional CTA: “Ask for a pilot plan template / benchmark checklist”

Audience Q&A prompts (seed questions for the moderator)
Security / compliance
- What exactly is included in your SOC 2 scope and how do we obtain the report?
- Do you support customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM) and what’s required from our side?
- What do your audit logs capture, and can we export them to our SIEM?
- Can we configure retention for prompts/outputs/metadata? What are default retention assumptions?
- What identity options do you support (SAML/SSO, RBAC, SCIM)? Any known edge cases?

Architecture / operations
- What’s the practical difference between Dedicated and Private for regulated workloads?
- How do rollouts/canaries work when we need strict change control?
- How do you handle failover/region isolation for regulated traffic?
- What does a typical onboarding look like for VPC deployments?

Performance / economics
- How should we think about cost controls without sacrificing latency?
- What workload patterns benefit most from batching and caching?
- What metrics do you recommend we track for production (p95, error rate, token cost)?

Procurement / rollout
- How long does a typical security review + procurement cycle take?
- Do you offer an AWS Marketplace private offer path?

Promotion plan (GTM)
Targeting
- Primary: VP/Director Security, CISO staff, Head of Platform Eng, ML Platform Lead, Principal/Staff Eng in data/AI platform
- Secondary: Compliance, VRM, procurement (as optional invite)

Channels
1) Email (ABM list + house list)
- 2 invitation waves + 2 reminders + last-day reminder
- Add a “request evidence pack” CTA in confirmation email

2) LinkedIn
- Organic posts from speakers (Camila, Markus, Aisha)
- Paid: job title targeting (note low volume; focus on NA + UK + DACH)
- Creative: “Audit-ready inference” / “VPC + audit logs + predictable throughput”

3) Partner channel
- AWS co-sell: invite targeted accounts flagged for Marketplace motion
- Optional: invite security consultants / GRC partners (if we have a list)

4) On-site
- Banner on regulated workloads landing page
- Exit-intent modal only for regulated page traffic (avoid site-wide spam)

Creative + copy notes
- Keep claims scoped; include footnote: “Security capabilities vary by deployment mode; details provided during security review.”

Operational checklist
- Landing page: event registration + calendar invites + UTM tracking (Tyler)
- Speaker prep: 30-min dry run; Q&A run-through; compliance review of slides (Soojin + Naomi + Elliot)
- Demo: run in staging; ensure no customer data; record backup
- Lead routing: registrants matched to target accounts; assign AE owner; book meeting CTA routes to scheduler

Follow-up plan (post-webinar)
Timeline
- T+0 hours: “Thanks + recording + slides (if approved)” email
- T+1 day: Sales/SDR outreach to high-intent attendees (asked questions, clicked CTA)
- T+3 days: Nurture email with “security FAQ” + “pilot checklist”
- T+7 days: Breakup email + offer: “30-min security questionnaire triage”

Scoring signals (for ABM ops)
- Attended live + asked a question (high)
- Clicked “request evidence pack” (very high)
- Clicked “book security review” (very high)
- Registered only (medium)
- No show + no clicks (low)
